title: Add probabilistic early-expiration to a Redis product cache to stop a daily stampede
business_context: An e-commerce catalog service serves 18k req/s for the homepage 'featured products' block. Each cache key has a 60s TTL. Every 60s all keys expire near-simultaneously and ~12k requests miss at once, hammering Postgres with identical aggregation queries; p99 spikes from 40ms to 2.1s and the DB CPU saturates at 95%.
constraints: Must stay backward compatible with existing get/set call sites (no signature changes for callers)
12 stateless API instances share one Redis cluster; no per-instance coordination allowed
Aggregation query takes 700ms; only one recompute per key should run cluster-wide
Cannot raise TTL beyond 120s (merchandising freshness SLA)
Redis memory budget is fixed; no extra keys-per-product beyond a single lock key
p99 under load must stay below 150ms

before_state.description: Plain read-through cache with a fixed TTL. All keys for the featured block are written in the same request, so they expire together and every instance recomputes simultaneously.

issues: No stampede protection: on expiry every concurrent miss runs aggregateFeatured
Fixed 60s TTL with synchronized writes guarantees correlated expiry
No stored timestamp to reason about recompute timing
Directly fronts the cache; a miss storm propagates straight to DB
No degraded mode if DB is slow

reasoning: Naive fix: just bump the TTL. Rejected because freshness SLA caps TTL at 120s and a longer TTL only delays the same synchronized stampede. Alternative: a global mutex (SETNX lock) so one instance recomputes while others block. Rejected as the sole fix because under 12k concurrent misses thousands of requests would still stall on the lock, and a crashed lock holder risks a deadlock window. Chosen path: XFetch-style probabilistic early recomputation. We store value + delta (compute time) + expiry, and on read a request recomputes early with probability rising as expiry approaches (now - delta*beta*ln(rand) >= expiry). One request refreshes ahead of expiry while others serve the still-valid cached value. Cross-layer: UI/home route unchanged -> productCache.get adds early-recompute decision -> a short SETNX lock guards the actual recompute so only one wins -> DB sees ~1 query per key per window -> config exposes beta and TTL -> tests simulate 12k concurrent reads asserting <=1 DB call.

after_state.description: Cache stores value, delta and absolute expiry. Reads probabilistically trigger an early async recompute guarded by a per-key SETNX lock, so exactly one instance refreshes before expiry and no synchronized miss storm occurs.

files.purpose: Implements probabilistic early recomputation plus a per-key lock so one instance refreshes ahead of expiry
Hardened Redis client with bounded retries so a slow Redis cannot stall request threads indefinitely
Adds a degraded 503 path so a DB outage surfaces cleanly instead of hanging connections

files.content: module.exports = { CACHE_BETA: 1.0, CACHE_TTL: 90, LOCK_MS: 5000 };
files.purpose: Centralizes tunables so load tests and prod share the same knobs

migration_strategy: Deploy new code behind env flag CACHE_XFETCH=off so getFeatured still uses legacy fixed-TTL path
Enable CACHE_XFETCH on one canary instance; watch DB query count per window and p99
Ramp to 25% then 100% of instances over 2 hours while monitoring DB CPU
Tune BETA from 1.0 toward 1.5 if early recomputes are too rare during ramp
Remove legacy fixed-TTL branch once 100% stable for 48h
Rollback: flip CACHE_XFETCH=off; old read-through path is still present and reversible instantly
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds Redis with a key whose exp is 1s away and delta=0.7. Spawn 12k concurrent getFeatured calls against a stubbed db.aggregateFeatured that counts invocations. Assert output equals cached value for all callers AND db call count <= 1 (side-effect on DB). Negative/chaos test: make redis.set for the lock reject (simulated Redis blip) and assert callers still return last-known value rather than throwing, and that no caller triggers more than the single cold-path query.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With a fixed TTL and synchronized writes, all featured keys expire in the same millisecond; the next 12k concurrent reads each see a miss and each launch the 700ms aggregation, so the DB receives ~12k identical heavy queries within one window and saturates.

title: Introduce request coalescing in a NestJS pricing service to collapse duplicate in-flight DB calls
business_context: A fintech pricing API recomputes FX-adjusted quotes. During market open, a single instance receives ~800 concurrent requests for the same currency pair within 50ms. Each request independently misses the empty cache and calls the rate engine (120ms), so the instance issues 800 identical upstream calls, exhausting the upstream connection pool and tripping its rate limiter.
constraints: Single-instance coalescing only; cross-instance handled separately by Redis TTL
Quote must be at most 250ms stale (regulatory pricing freshness)
Upstream rate engine allows max 50 concurrent connections per client
No global locks that could deadlock the event loop
Must preserve existing async getQuote(pair) signature
Coalescing must not leak memory if a key is never resolved

before_state.description: Each request that misses the in-memory cache independently calls the upstream rate engine. Concurrent misses for the same pair fan out into hundreds of duplicate upstream calls.

issues: No in-flight tracking: N concurrent misses make N upstream calls
Upstream pool exhausted at market open
Cache populated only after upstream returns, so the 120ms window is unprotected

reasoning: Naive approach: lower concurrency by adding a semaphore around the upstream so only 50 calls run at once. Rejected because the other 750 requests still queue behind the semaphore and each still performs a redundant upstream call once admitted, so duplicate work and latency remain. Chosen path: request coalescing (single-flight). Store a Promise per key in an in-flight map; concurrent callers for the same pair await the same Promise. Cross-layer: controller calls getQuote unchanged -> service checks fresh cache, else checks inFlight map -> if present awaits it, else creates the Promise, stores it, and clears it in finally -> upstream sees exactly one call per pair per 120ms window -> config keeps 200ms TTL under the 250ms SLA -> tests assert single upstream call under 800 concurrent callers.

after_state.description: A single-flight map ensures concurrent callers for the same pair share one upstream Promise; the entry is always cleared in finally to avoid leaks, and the fresh-cache fast path is preserved.

files.purpose: Single-flight coalescing collapses concurrent misses into one upstream call while keeping the fresh-cache fast path
Asserts both output correctness and the side-effect of exactly one upstream call

migration_strategy: Ship coalescing behind feature flag COALESCE_QUOTES=false defaulting to legacy path
Enable on one canary pod during a low-volume session; verify upstream call count drops
Enable across the fleet before next market open
Add a watchdog metric for inFlight map size to catch leaks
Remove the legacy non-coalesced branch after one trading week
Rollback: set COALESCE_QUOTES=false to restore independent fetches instantly
testing_strategy: Production-shaped fixture: 800 concurrent getQuote('EURUSD') against an upstream stub that sleeps 120ms and increments a counter. Assert all 800 results are identical AND counter === 1. Chaos test: make the upstream Promise reject for one pair and assert the inFlight entry is removed (map size returns to 0) so a subsequent call retries rather than returning a stuck rejected Promise.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A semaphore caps concurrency but does not deduplicate: each admitted request still issues its own upstream call, so 800 distinct calls eventually run and the rate engine's limiter still trips while latency balloons behind the queue.

title: Fix stale-after-write cache invalidation in a Django inventory service using versioned keys
business_context: A logistics SaaS caches warehouse stock counts in Redis with a 5-minute TTL. After a stock adjustment, customers see the old count for up to 5 minutes, causing overselling: ~30 oversold orders per day that require manual cancellation and refunds averaging $42 each.
constraints: Writes and reads come from separate services sharing one Redis
Cannot lower TTL below 5 minutes (read load would 4x the DB)
Invalidation must be safe under concurrent write + read interleavings
Backward compatible: existing get_stock(sku) callers unchanged
No distributed transaction across Postgres and Redis available
Must avoid the classic delete-then-set race that re-caches stale data

before_state.description: On write the code updates the DB then deletes the cache key. A concurrent read between the DB commit and delete can repopulate the cache with stale data, and even correct deletes leave a 5-minute stale window if the delete is skipped on the write path.

files.content:
import json
from .redis import r

def get_stock(sku):
    key = f'stock:{sku}'
    hit = r.get(key)
    if hit:
        return json.loads(hit)
    val = db_fetch_stock(sku)
    r.set(key, json.dumps(val), ex=300)
    return val

def adjust_stock(sku, delta):
    db_adjust(sku, delta)
    # invalidation sometimes missing on certain write paths
    r.delete(f'stock:{sku}')
issues: delete-then-set race: a read between db_adjust commit and r.delete can re-cache the old value
Some write paths (bulk import) never call adjust_stock so the cache is never invalidated
No way to detect a stale cached entry vs current

reasoning: Naive approach: just delete the key on every write. Rejected because the delete-after-DB-commit pattern has a well-known race: a concurrent read that loaded the old value just before the commit can write it back after the delete, restoring stale data; and bulk import paths bypass the single write function. Alternative: write-through (update cache inside the write) — rejected because multiple write services would need Redis credentials and the cache would diverge if a write succeeds in DB but fails in Redis. Chosen path: monotonic version pointer per SKU. A small 'ver:sku' integer is bumped on any DB write (cheap, atomic INCR). The cache key embeds the version; reads first fetch the current version then read the value at that version. A stale value simply becomes unreferenced and TTLs out. Cross-layer: read path get_stock fetches ver then value:sku:ver -> write path runs db_adjust and INCR ver atomically -> bulk import bumps ver once at end -> no delete race because old versions are never read again -> tests assert read-after-write returns fresh value even with an interleaved concurrent read.

after_state.description: Each SKU has a monotonic version counter. Reads resolve the current version then read the versioned value key. Any write bumps the version, instantly orphaning stale entries without a fragile delete.

files.purpose: Closes the bulk-import gap by bumping the version pointer for every imported SKU

files.content:
import redis, os
r = redis.Redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'], socket_timeout=0.2, socket_connect_timeout=0.2)
files.purpose: Bounded socket timeouts so a Redis hiccup falls back to DB rather than hanging request workers

files.content:
def test_read_after_write_is_fresh(redis_clean):
    seed_db('SKU1', 10)
    assert get_stock('SKU1') == 10
    adjust_stock('SKU1', -3)  # db now 7, version bumped
    assert get_stock('SKU1') == 7

def test_concurrent_read_does_not_recache_stale(redis_clean):
    seed_db('SKU1', 10)
    v = _ver('SKU1')
    adjust_stock('SKU1', -3)  # version moved to v+1
    # simulate a slow reader that loaded old version v
    r.set(f'stock:SKU1:{v}', json.dumps(10))
    assert get_stock('SKU1') == 7  # reads v+1, ignores orphaned v
files.purpose: Verifies freshness after write and that orphaned old-version entries are never served

migration_strategy: Deploy version-aware read/write with ver counters initialized lazily to 0
Backfill bump_version for all SKUs touched by recent writes via a one-off job
Run new and old key schemes in parallel for one TTL window so warm caches drain
Switch reads fully to versioned keys after the parallel window
Delete legacy stock:sku keys via SCAN once traffic shows zero hits
Rollback: read path can fall back to legacy stock:sku key if ver lookups error
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds Postgres with SKU1=10 and a clean Redis. Assert get_stock returns 10, then adjust_stock(-3) and assert get_stock returns 7 (output) and that ver:SKU1 incremented (side-effect). Chaos test: inject a stale orphaned entry at the old version and a concurrent reader, asserting the reader never serves the orphaned value. Negative test: simulate Redis socket timeout and assert get_stock still returns the DB value.
what_breaks_naive_approach: delete-then-set leaves a window where a concurrent reader that loaded the pre-commit value writes it back into the cache after the delete, so the stale count survives for the full 5-minute TTL and overselling continues; bulk imports that bypass the single write function never invalidate at all.

title: Add a two-tier (in-process LRU + Redis) cache to a Go feature-flag service to cut Redis QPS
business_context: A B2B SaaS evaluates feature flags on every request. The Go API hits Redis once per flag per request: at 25k req/s with ~8 flags each, that is 200k Redis GETs/s, pushing the Redis node to 80% CPU and adding 1.5ms network RTT per call to p99. Flags change a few times per hour.
constraints: Flag changes must propagate within 5 seconds across all instances
40 instances; in-process caches must converge after a flag edit
Memory per instance for L1 capped at 50MB
Existing Eval(ctx, flag, user) API must not change
No per-request Redis call in the hot path once warm
Thread-safe under heavy goroutine concurrency

before_state.description: Every flag evaluation does a Redis GET. There is no in-process layer, so identical flag reads repeat across millions of requests and Redis becomes the bottleneck.

issues: One Redis round-trip per flag per request
No local caching despite flags changing only a few times/hour
Redis CPU and network RTT dominate p99

reasoning: Naive approach: add a per-instance map cache with a short TTL (e.g. 5s). Rejected because TTL-only invalidation means after a flag edit, half the fleet keeps the stale value for up to 5s with no guarantee of bounded skew, and a sync expiry across 40 instances re-stampedes Redis. Alternative: long-poll Redis per flag — rejected, too many connections. Chosen path: two-tier cache (L1 in-process LRU, L2 Redis) with active invalidation via Redis Pub/Sub. L1 serves the hot path with no network call. On a flag edit, the writer publishes the flag name on a channel; every instance subscribes and evicts that key from L1, then lazily reloads from L2. Cross-layer: Eval hits L1 -> miss falls to L2 Redis -> writer updates Redis and PUBLISH flag-changed -> subscriber goroutine evicts L1 entry -> bounded 50MB LRU -> tests assert zero Redis calls when warm and convergence within the pub/sub latency.

after_state.description: L1 in-process LRU serves warm reads with no network. L2 Redis is the source of truth. A Pub/Sub subscriber evicts L1 entries on flag edits so all instances converge within sub-second pub/sub latency.

files.purpose: Hot path served from L1; only L1 misses touch Redis
Active invalidation: writes publish a change event that evicts the key on every instance
Bounded thread-safe LRU enforcing the 50MB memory cap
Asserts the warm hot path makes zero Redis calls

migration_strategy: Deploy L1+invalidator with L1 disabled via flag L1_ENABLED=false (pass-through to Redis)
Enable L1 on 2 canary instances; verify Redis QPS drops and flag edits still propagate
Confirm pub/sub eviction by editing a flag and measuring convergence time
Ramp L1_ENABLED to 100% over an hour watching Redis CPU fall
Add alert if any instance misses pub/sub heartbeats (eviction safety)
Rollback: set L1_ENABLED=false fleet-wide to revert to direct Redis reads
testing_strategy: Fixture wires a counting Redis stub returning 'on'. Warm L1 with one Eval, then 1000 Evals and assert zero additional Redis GET calls (side-effect) and correct boolean output. Convergence test: two Service instances sharing a fake pub/sub; SetFlag on one and assert the other evicts and reloads the new value. Chaos test: drop the pub/sub channel mid-run and assert a configurable safety TTL on L1 still bounds staleness so no key is stale beyond 5s.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A TTL-only L1 gives unbounded per-instance staleness up to the TTL after a flag edit and, worse, synchronized TTL expiry across 40 instances causes a coordinated Redis read burst — re-creating a stampede at the L2 layer that the cache was meant to remove.

title: Replace cache-aside with negative caching to stop a DB hammer from missing-key lookups
business_context: A social platform resolves @handles to user IDs via a cache-aside lookup. Bots probe thousands of non-existent handles per second. Because misses are never cached, every probe for a nonexistent handle becomes a DB query: ~9k QPS of pure miss traffic, with Postgres index scans on the handles table driving its CPU to 70%.
constraints: Negative entries must expire faster than positive ones (a handle may be registered later)
Must not let an attacker fill Redis with unbounded negative keys (memory DoS)
Handle registration must immediately invalidate a negative entry
Existing resolveHandle(handle) -> userId|null contract preserved
Resolution latency p99 must stay under 20ms
Cache and DB are shared by 8 instances

before_state.description: Only successful lookups are cached. Misses fall through to the DB every time, so a flood of nonexistent-handle probes bypasses the cache entirely.

issues: Misses are never cached, so repeated probes for the same nonexistent handle re-query the DB
Bot traffic of nonexistent handles bypasses the cache entirely
No protection against unbounded distinct probes

reasoning: Naive approach: cache misses with the same TTL as hits. Rejected because attackers can probe millions of distinct random handles and fill Redis memory (a cache-poisoning DoS), and a long negative TTL would hide a handle that gets registered seconds later. Alternative: a Bloom filter of existing handles to short-circuit misses — rejected as the sole fix because handle registration mutates the set constantly and a stale Bloom filter would wrongly reject valid handles. Chosen path: negative caching with a short TTL plus a bound. Cache a sentinel for misses with a 30s TTL (vs 600s for hits), key the negative entries so registration deletes them, and cap negative memory by relying on Redis maxmemory-policy volatile-ttl so short-lived negatives are evicted first. Cross-layer: resolveHandle stores a NEG sentinel on miss -> registration path deletes the key on signup -> short negative TTL bounds staleness for newly registered handles -> Redis eviction policy bounds memory -> tests assert repeated misses hit DB once and that registration clears the negative entry.

after_state.description: Misses are cached with a short-lived sentinel that bounds repeated DB lookups, while registration deletes the sentinel so a freshly registered handle resolves immediately. Redis eviction policy caps negative-key memory.

files.purpose: Adds negative caching with a short TTL and a sentinel to absorb repeated miss probes
Invalidates the negative entry on registration so a new handle resolves without waiting for TTL

files.content:
maxmemory 2gb
maxmemory-policy volatile-ttl
files.purpose: Bounds memory and evicts short-TTL negative keys first, preventing a memory-DoS via probing
Asserts a single DB hit per repeated miss and immediate resolution after registration

migration_strategy: Set redis maxmemory-policy to volatile-ttl in a maintenance window (verify no positive keys rely on noeviction)
Deploy negative caching behind NEG_CACHE=false; resolve path still skips negatives
Enable NEG_CACHE on canary; confirm DB miss QPS drops and no valid handle is wrongly returned null
Verify registration invalidation by signing up a probed handle and resolving it
Ramp to 100% and watch Postgres CPU fall
Rollback: NEG_CACHE=false stops writing sentinels; existing short-TTL negatives drain in 30s
testing_strategy: Fixture stubs db.userByHandle counting calls. Probe the same nonexistent handle twice; assert both return null AND dbCalls === 1 (side-effect bound). Registration test: cache a negative, register, assert next resolve returns the id and the sentinel key was deleted. Chaos test: simulate 100k distinct random-handle probes and assert Redis memory stays under maxmemory (eviction works) and no positive key was evicted.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain cache-aside never stores misses, so each repeated probe of a nonexistent handle re-runs the DB index scan; a bot issuing the same or many missing handles sustains ~9k miss QPS straight to Postgres regardless of how warm the positive cache is.

title: Add stale-while-revalidate to a Next.js CDN edge cache to eliminate origin latency spikes
business_context: A media site renders article pages via Next.js on Vercel. Pages are cached at the edge for 60s. On expiry the next visitor triggers a blocking origin render (900ms cold) while holding the connection; during peak this causes ~4% of requests to see >1s TTFB and a measurable bounce-rate increase of 6% on those slow loads.
constraints: Article content may change but 60s of staleness is acceptable to readers
Must work across all 70+ Vercel edge regions without per-region coordination
Origin render budget: keep concurrent origin renders per article <= 1
Editorial publish must purge within 10 seconds
No blocking origin fetch on the user-facing request path
Backward compatible with existing getStaticProps data shape

before_state.description: Edge cache uses a plain max-age. When an entry expires, the next request blocks on a full origin render before any bytes are sent, so expiry directly translates into user-visible latency spikes.

issues: On revalidation the requesting user can wait for the full origin render
No stale-while-revalidate header so the edge cannot serve stale during regeneration
Publish purge relies solely on the 60s timer

reasoning: Naive approach: shorten revalidate so content is fresher. Rejected because a shorter window increases the frequency of blocking regenerations and makes the latency spikes more common, not fewer. Alternative: pre-render every article on publish (full static export) — rejected because the catalog has 400k articles and most get little traffic, so eagerly rendering all of them wastes build minutes and storage. Chosen path: explicit stale-while-revalidate (SWR) caching headers plus on-demand revalidation on publish. The edge serves the stale page instantly to the user and revalidates in the background; a publish webhook calls res.revalidate to purge within seconds. Cross-layer: page sets Cache-Control s-maxage=60, stale-while-revalidate=300 -> edge serves stale and refreshes async -> publish webhook hits /api/revalidate -> origin regenerates once -> readers never block -> tests assert background revalidation does not block the response and publish purges quickly.

after_state.description: Edge serves stale content instantly while revalidating in the background, and a publish webhook triggers on-demand revalidation so edits propagate within seconds instead of waiting for the timer.

files.purpose: On-demand generation for the long tail with ISR keeping pages warm
Adds the stale-while-revalidate edge header so users get stale instantly while the edge refreshes in the background
Authenticated on-demand purge so editorial publishes propagate within seconds
Asserts the user response is fast and a single background revalidation occurred

migration_strategy: Deploy middleware SWR header to a preview environment and verify edge behavior
Roll to 5% of traffic via Vercel split and watch TTFB p99 on revalidation
Wire the publish webhook to /api/revalidate in staging, confirm <10s purge
Ramp SWR to 100% of article routes
Decommission reliance on the bare 60s timer for freshness once webhook proven
Rollback: remove the stale-while-revalidate directive from middleware to revert to blocking ISR
testing_strategy: Fixture primes an edge entry, advances time past s-maxage into the SWR window, then issues a request and asserts response time < 50ms (output: not blocked) while a background origin render counter increments by exactly one (side-effect). Publish test: call /api/revalidate with a valid token and assert revalidated:true and that the next request reflects new content. Chaos test: make the origin render throw during background revalidation and assert the edge keeps serving the last good stale copy rather than erroring the user.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With plain max-age and no SWR, the first request after expiry must wait for the 900ms cold origin render before any byte is returned; shortening the TTL only makes these blocking regenerations more frequent, worsening the TTFB tail under peak load.

title: Eliminate a thundering-herd on cold Redis restart in a gaming leaderboard using jittered TTLs and warmup
business_context: A mobile game shows a global leaderboard cached in Redis. After a Redis failover the cache is empty; 60k concurrent players load the leaderboard at once and every request runs the expensive ranked aggregation (Postgres window function, 1.2s). The DB instantly saturates and the API returns 504s for ~90 seconds until the cache warms.
constraints: Failover must not cascade into a DB outage
Leaderboard aggregation is heavy (1.2s) and cannot be made cheaper short term
Must support 60k concurrent cold reads
Single recompute cluster-wide on cold start
TTLs must be jittered to avoid future synchronized expiry
No change to the GET /leaderboard response schema

before_state.description: Cold cache plus synchronized client load means every request recomputes the leaderboard simultaneously. There is no warmup and no single-flight, so a failover becomes a DB outage.

issues: No single-flight: cold start runs 60k simultaneous 1.2s aggregations
Fixed 30s TTL with no jitter causes synchronized expiry storms
No warmup after failover

reasoning: Naive approach: add a SETNX lock around the recompute. That helps duplicate work but rejected as the sole fix because on a fully cold cache 60k requests would all block on the lock for 1.2s, blowing the request timeout and connection pool. Alternative: serve an empty leaderboard during cold start — rejected, unacceptable UX for a flagship screen. Chosen path: combine single-flight recompute, serve-stale-on-lock-wait, a proactive warmup task triggered on Redis ready/failover, and jittered TTLs. The lock ensures one recompute; waiters return last-known-good or a brief 'building' state; a startup/health hook pre-warms the key so failover never presents a fully cold cache to users. Cross-layer: getLeaderboard checks cache -> on miss tries lock -> lock holder recomputes, others get stale or 'building' -> redis 'ready' event triggers warmup -> jittered TTL spreads future expiries -> tests assert one aggregation under 60k cold reads.

after_state.description: A single-flight lock guarantees one recompute; waiters serve last-known-good. A warmup hook repopulates the key on Redis reconnect, and TTLs carry jitter so expiries never synchronize again.

files.purpose: Single-flight recompute with jittered TTL and a long-lived stale fallback for waiters
Repopulates the leaderboard on Redis reconnect/failover so users never face a fully cold cache
Returns a non-blocking 202 building status during the brief cold window instead of a 504
Asserts the single-flight lock collapses a cold herd into one aggregation

migration_strategy: Deploy single-flight + stale fallback behind LB_SINGLEFLIGHT=false
Enable on canary and run a controlled FLUSHDB on a staging Redis to simulate failover
Verify exactly one aggregation runs and waiters get stale or 202
Enable warmup hook and confirm key repopulates within seconds of reconnect
Ramp to 100% and add jittered TTL; watch expiry distribution flatten
Rollback: LB_SINGLEFLIGHT=false reverts to direct recompute path
testing_strategy: Fixture stubs db.rankedAggregate with a 1.2s delay and a call counter. Launch 60k concurrent getLeaderboard with an empty Redis and assert aggCalls === 1 (side-effect). Assert waiters receive stale data when present. Chaos test: emit a Redis 'ready' event mid-traffic to simulate failover and assert warmup repopulates lb:global and that no more than one aggregation runs during the failover window.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A bare SETNX lock prevents duplicate computation but on a cold cache all 60k requests block on the lock for the full 1.2s recompute, exceeding request and pool timeouts and still returning 504s; without jitter the next synchronized expiry repeats the storm.

title: Fix cache key cardinality explosion from unnormalized query params in an Express search cache
business_context: A travel marketplace caches search results keyed by the raw query string. Because clients append params in arbitrary order and include irrelevant tracking params (utm_*, _t timestamps), logically identical searches produce millions of distinct keys. Hit rate sits at 11%, Redis memory hit its 8GB limit and started evicting hot keys, and the DB carries far more search load than it should.
constraints: Logically equivalent searches must share one cache key
Tracking params must never affect the cache key
Key normalization must be stable and order-independent
Must not collide distinct searches into the same key
Backward compatible: existing search(params) API unchanged
Redis memory must drop below 4GB after rollout

before_state.description: Cache key is the raw query string. Param order and tracking params create many keys for one logical search, exploding cardinality and crushing hit rate.

issues: Key includes param order and tracking params, so identical searches make distinct keys
Hit rate ~11%; Redis at memory limit evicting hot keys
No allowlist of cache-relevant params

reasoning: Naive approach: strip a hardcoded list of known tracking params. Rejected because the tracking-param set grows over time and a blocklist always lags, so new utm-like params keep leaking into keys. Alternative: cache by a hash of the full parsed object — rejected because it still includes irrelevant params and is order-sensitive unless sorted. Chosen path: an allowlist-based canonicalization. Define the exact set of params that semantically affect results (origin, dest, dates, pax, cabin, sort), extract only those, normalize values (lowercase, sorted lists, ISO dates), serialize in sorted key order, and hash to a fixed-length key. Cross-layer: search route passes raw params -> canonicalize() projects to the allowlist and normalizes -> stable hash forms the key -> Redis stores one entry per logical search -> tests assert param-order and tracking-param variants map to the same key while genuinely different searches differ.

after_state.description: Keys are derived from an allowlist of result-affecting params, normalized and sorted, then hashed. Logically identical searches collapse to one key; tracking params and order are irrelevant.

files.purpose: Projects params to the allowlist, normalizes and sorts them, and produces a stable key hash
Uses the canonical key so equivalent searches share one cache entry
Asserts collapse of equivalent searches and separation of distinct ones

migration_strategy: Deploy canonicalize behind CANON_KEYS=false (still uses raw key)
Log canonical-vs-raw keys in shadow mode to verify no unintended collisions
Enable CANON_KEYS on canary; watch hit rate climb and memory fall
Ramp to 100% over a day; old raw keys TTL out within 5 minutes
Verify Redis memory under 4GB then re-enable hot-key safety alerts
Rollback: CANON_KEYS=false reverts to raw-key behavior immediately
testing_strategy: Fixture provides param permutations of one logical search (reordered, with utm_* and _t) and asserts canonicalize returns one identical key (output) and that only one Redis SET occurs across the permutations (side-effect). Separation test: distinct destinations produce distinct keys. Chaos/negative test: a request with only tracking params and no allowlisted params produces a stable empty-allowlist key and does not collide with a real search.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A hardcoded tracking-param blocklist always trails new param names, so freshly added marketing params re-explode key cardinality; raw-string keys also remain order-sensitive, so the same search typed two ways still misses, keeping hit rate near 11% and memory pinned at the eviction threshold.

title: Replace cache-aside with write-through + read-through for a healthcare patient-summary cache to fix split-brain
business_context: A healthcare portal caches patient summary documents. With cache-aside, a clinician updates a summary in the DB but a separate cache invalidation step occasionally fails (network blip), leaving clinicians reading a stale summary for the 15-minute TTL. In a regulated setting, showing outdated medication lists is a patient-safety incident; 3 near-misses were reported last quarter.
constraints: Stale clinical data is unacceptable: read-after-write must be consistent
Cache and DB writes must not both succeed-or-both-fail without a transaction outbox
Must remain available if Redis is down (degrade to DB)
Audit log must record every summary write
Existing getSummary(id) and saveSummary(id, doc) signatures preserved
PHI must not be logged

before_state.description: Cache-aside with a best-effort invalidate on write. If the invalidate fails, the stale summary persists for the full TTL, which is a clinical-safety risk.

issues: Invalidation is best-effort; a failed delete leaves stale clinical data for 15 minutes
No read-after-write consistency guarantee
No audit trail tied to the write

reasoning: Naive approach: retry the cache delete a few times. Rejected because retries still fail in a partition and the failure mode (stale clinical data) is unacceptable even at low probability. Alternative: drop the cache entirely for summaries — rejected because read load would 5x the document store and breach the portal's latency SLO. Chosen path: write-through cache fronting the DB inside a transactional outbox. saveSummary writes the DB row and an outbox event in one transaction; a worker applies the write-through to Redis and records the audit entry; reads are read-through. If Redis is unavailable, reads bypass to the DB so the system degrades safely. Cross-layer: UI saves -> saveSummary writes DB row + outbox row atomically -> outbox worker updates Redis (write-through) and appends audit -> getSummary reads cache, else DB, and on DB read refreshes cache -> tests assert read-after-write returns the new doc even if the initial cache update is delayed.

after_state.description: Writes are committed to the DB and an outbox in one transaction; a worker performs the write-through to Redis and writes the audit log. Reads are read-through and degrade to the DB if Redis is unavailable, guaranteeing no stale clinical data.

files.purpose: Read-through with DB fallback; writes are atomic with an outbox event

files.content:
def process_outbox():
    for ev in db.fetch_unprocessed_outbox('summary_written'):
        pid = ev['payload']['pid']
        doc = db.fetch_summary(pid)
        r.set(f'sum:{pid}', json.dumps(doc), ex=900)  # write-through
        audit.log('summary_write', patient_ref=pid)   # no PHI in audit
        db.mark_outbox_done(ev['id'])
files.purpose: Applies the write-through and audit reliably with retry semantics until the outbox row is marked done
Records every write without persisting protected health information

files.content:
def test_read_after_write_consistent(clean):
    save_summary('p1', {'meds': ['A']})
    process_outbox()
    assert get_summary('p1')['meds'] == ['A']

def test_stale_not_served_if_cache_update_pending(clean):
    seed_cache('p1', {'meds': ['OLD']})
    save_summary('p1', {'meds': ['NEW']})
    # before worker runs, a read must not return stale clinical data
    # outbox worker invalidates first
    invalidate_pending('p1')
    assert get_summary('p1')['meds'] == ['NEW']
files.purpose: Asserts read-after-write consistency and that pending updates never serve stale data

migration_strategy: Create the outbox table and worker; deploy with worker disabled and writes still doing best-effort delete
Enable outbox writes in shadow (write event but keep legacy delete) to validate the worker
Switch save_summary to outbox-only invalidation on canary patients
Verify audit entries and read-after-write consistency on canary
Ramp to all patients; remove the legacy best-effort delete
Rollback: re-enable legacy delete path and disable worker if outbox lag appears
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds DB and a clean Redis. save_summary then process_outbox; assert get_summary returns the new doc (output) and an audit row exists with no PHI (side-effect). Consistency test: pre-seed a stale cache value, save a new doc, run the invalidation step, and assert no stale value is served. Chaos test: take Redis offline and assert get_summary still returns the correct DB document and that the outbox worker retries the write-through once Redis returns.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Best-effort delete with retries still fails under a network partition; because the failure leaves the stale summary cached for the full 15-minute TTL, a clinician can read an outdated medication list, which is the exact patient-safety incident the team must eliminate.

title: Add tag-based bulk invalidation to a FastAPI catalog cache to fix slow, incomplete category purges
business_context: A retail API caches product detail JSON per SKU. When a category-wide price change lands (e.g. a 20% sale on 8,000 SKUs), ops must invalidate all affected keys. Today they SCAN the keyspace with a pattern, which blocks Redis for ~4s and frequently misses keys created mid-scan, so a fraction of products show old prices during a flash sale.
constraints: Bulk invalidation of up to 50k keys must not block Redis
Invalidation must be complete (no missed keys) even with concurrent writes
A SKU can belong to multiple categories (multi-tag)
Existing get_product(sku) API unchanged
Invalidation latency target: under 2 seconds for a category
Must work across a Redis Cluster (no cross-slot SCAN guarantees)

before_state.description: Per-SKU keys with no grouping. Category invalidation uses SCAN MATCH which is O(keyspace), blocks Redis, and races with concurrent writes so newly created keys are missed.

issues: SCAN MATCH over the whole keyspace blocks Redis and is O(N)
Keys created during the scan are missed -> partial invalidation
Per-key db.product call inside the loop is extremely slow

reasoning: Naive approach: keep SCAN but run it in smaller batches with COUNT. Rejected because batching reduces blocking slightly but still scans the entire keyspace, is O(N), and still races with concurrent writes so it remains incomplete. Alternative: maintain a Redis SET of all SKUs per category and delete each member — rejected as the primary mechanism because deleting 8k keys plus set maintenance under concurrent writes is racy and cross-slot in a cluster. Chosen path: tag-based versioned invalidation. Each category tag holds a generation counter; product keys embed the generations of their tags. Invalidating a category is a single INCR on its tag generation, instantly orphaning every product keyed under the old generation. Reads compose the current tag generations into the key. Cross-layer: get_product reads tag gens then the composed key -> invalidate_category INCRs the tag gen (O(1), no scan) -> stale entries orphan and TTL out -> works per-slot in cluster since each op is single-key -> tests assert a category INCR makes all member SKUs miss and that concurrent writes are not lost.

after_state.description: Each category tag has a generation counter. Product cache keys embed the generations of the SKU's tags, so a single INCR per category orphans every affected key instantly without scanning, complete even under concurrent writes.

files.purpose: Resolves and bumps per-category generation counters used to compose cache keys

files.content:
from .tags import tag_gens

def get_product(sku):
    doc_cats = db.product_categories(sku)  # cheap, separately cached
    gen = tag_gens(doc_cats)
    key = f'prod:{sku}:{gen}'
    hit = r.get(key)
    if hit:
        return json.loads(hit)
    doc = db.product(sku)
    r.set(key, json.dumps(doc), ex=600)
    return doc
files.purpose: Composes the cache key from current tag generations so bumping a tag orphans all its members
Replaces the O(N) blocking SCAN with one constant-time generation bump

files.content:
def test_category_bump_invalidates_all_members(clean):
    seed_categories('SKU1', ['sale'])
    seed_categories('SKU2', ['sale'])
    get_product('SKU1'); get_product('SKU2')  # warm
    invalidate_category('sale')
    # new reads use new gen -> old entries orphaned
    assert key_for('SKU1') != old_key('SKU1')

def test_concurrent_write_not_lost(clean):
    seed_categories('SKU3', ['sale'])
    invalidate_category('sale')
    get_product('SKU3')  # caches under new gen
    # a write that created a key mid-invalidation still maps to new gen
    assert r.exists(key_for('SKU3'))
files.purpose: Asserts complete invalidation via tag bump and no lost concurrent writes

migration_strategy: Introduce tag:gen counters initialized to 0 and the generation-aware key scheme behind TAG_KEYS=false
Warm tag generations and shadow-read both key schemes to verify parity
Enable TAG_KEYS on canary; verify category invalidation is instant and complete
Replace ops SCAN tooling with the bump endpoint
Ramp to 100%; legacy prod:sku keys TTL out within 10 minutes
Rollback: TAG_KEYS=false reverts to the legacy key/scan path
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds two SKUs in category 'sale', warms both, then calls invalidate_category('sale') and asserts subsequent reads compute a new composed key (output) and that no SCAN command was issued (side-effect, via a command interceptor). Completeness test: create a key during invalidation and assert it maps to the new generation. Chaos test: run invalidate_category concurrently with 500 get_product calls and assert every post-bump read resolves the new generation with zero stale hits.
what_breaks_naive_approach: SCAN MATCH walks the entire keyspace, blocking Redis for seconds and, critically, missing any key created after the cursor passed its slot — so during a flash sale a subset of products keeps the old price, exactly the incomplete-invalidation failure that loses revenue and trust.

title: Cache GraphQL resolver results with a DataLoader to kill an N+1 cache-miss storm
business_context: A B2B dashboard's GraphQL API resolves an org's 200 projects, each needing an owner User. The owner resolver does a cache-aside lookup per project, so one dashboard load fires 200 sequential Redis GETs and, on a cold cache, 200 DB queries. p95 for the dashboard query is 1.4s and the DB sees needless repeated lookups for shared owners.
constraints: Must batch within a single GraphQL request lifecycle
Owners are frequently shared across projects (dedupe required)
Cache layer (Redis) must still front the batched DB query
Per-request DataLoader instance to avoid cross-request cache poisoning
Existing schema and resolver signatures unchanged
p95 dashboard query under 300ms warm

before_state.description: The owner resolver performs an independent cache-aside lookup per project. There is no batching or per-request dedupe, producing an N+1 of Redis and DB calls.

issues: N+1: one Redis GET and possibly one DB query per project
No dedupe for shared owners
Sequential awaits inflate p95

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap each lookup in Promise.all in the parent resolver. Rejected because it parallelizes but does not dedupe shared owners and still issues N Redis/DB calls. Alternative: a process-wide cache map — rejected because it leaks data across requests and tenants (cross-request poisoning, an auth risk). Chosen path: a per-request DataLoader that batches owner IDs, MGETs them from Redis in one round-trip, fetches only the misses from the DB in one batched query, backfills Redis, and dedupes identical IDs automatically. Cross-layer: GraphQL context creates a fresh DataLoader per request -> owner resolver calls loader.load(ownerId) -> loader batches a tick of IDs -> single Redis MGET -> single DB IN-query for misses -> backfill cache -> tests assert one MGET and one DB batch for 200 projects.

after_state.description: A per-request DataLoader batches all owner IDs into a single Redis MGET and a single batched DB query for misses, deduping shared owners and eliminating the N+1.

files.purpose: Batches and dedupes owner lookups into one Redis MGET and one DB IN-query, backfilling cache
Resolver delegates to the per-request loader, removing per-project Redis/DB calls
Creates a per-request loader to prevent cross-request/tenant cache poisoning
Asserts single Redis MGET and single DB batch with dedupe across shared owners

migration_strategy: Add the loader and context wiring behind USE_LOADER=false (resolver keeps cache-aside)
Enable USE_LOADER on canary; verify MGET/DB call counts drop in traces
Confirm per-request isolation with a multi-tenant test in staging
Ramp to 100% and watch dashboard p95 fall
Remove the legacy per-project cache-aside resolver branch
Rollback: USE_LOADER=false restores the original resolver instantly
testing_strategy: Fixture stubs redis.mget and db.usersByIds with counters and 200 projects sharing 50 distinct owners. Assert correct owners returned (output) AND mgets === 1 and dbCalls === 1 (side-effects), proving batching and dedupe. Isolation test: two requests with different tenants get independent loaders and never share cached users. Chaos test: make redis.mget reject and assert the loader still resolves owners from the DB batch and surfaces the error per-key rather than failing the whole request unrelatedly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Promise.all parallelizes but does not dedupe, so 200 projects with 50 shared owners still issue 200 Redis lookups and up to 200 DB queries on a cold cache; a process-wide map would dedupe but leak one tenant's user data into another request, a correctness and auth failure.

title: Bound an unbounded in-memory cache causing OOM crashes in a Node image-metadata service
business_context: An image service caches EXIF metadata in a plain JS object keyed by image URL. The keyspace is effectively unbounded (millions of unique URLs/day), so the process heap grows until the container is OOM-killed every ~6 hours, dropping in-flight requests and triggering a restart storm across the fleet.
constraints: Memory per instance must stay under 512MB
Hot images (top ~5%) must retain high hit rate
Eviction must be O(1) amortized in the hot path
No external cache dependency for this layer (latency-critical)
Existing getMeta(url) signature unchanged
Must expose hit/miss/eviction metrics

before_state.description: An unbounded object cache grows without limit. Every distinct URL adds an entry that is never evicted, so the heap climbs until the process is OOM-killed.

issues: No size bound or eviction -> heap grows until OOM
Object keys for millions of URLs never freed
No metrics to see the leak

reasoning: Naive approach: periodically clear the whole object on a timer. Rejected because a full flush throws away hot entries too, causing a hit-rate cliff and a re-extraction storm right after each flush. Alternative: TTL each entry with setTimeout — rejected because millions of timers add their own memory and CPU overhead and do not bound total size. Chosen path: a bounded LRU with a fixed capacity and metrics. The LRU evicts the least-recently-used entry in O(1) when at capacity, keeping hot images resident while bounding memory. We add hit/miss/eviction counters for observability. Cross-layer: getMeta hits LRU -> miss extracts EXIF and inserts, evicting LRU tail if full -> capacity tuned to fit 512MB -> metrics exported to Prometheus -> tests assert capacity is enforced and hot keys survive churn.

after_state.description: A fixed-capacity LRU bounds memory and evicts cold entries in O(1), keeping hot images cached while preventing OOM. Hit/miss/eviction metrics make the cache observable.

files.purpose: Bounded LRU with TTL and eviction metrics replacing the unbounded object
Exposes hit/miss/eviction counters so the cache health is observable
Asserts the size bound and that recently used hot keys are retained

migration_strategy: Deploy LRU with capacity set conservatively low and metrics enabled
Observe heap and hit rate on canary for one churn cycle
Tune max upward until memory approaches but stays under 512MB
Ramp to fleet; confirm OOM kills stop
Add an alert on eviction rate spikes (capacity too small)
Rollback: revert to the object cache only if LRU shows unexpected hit-rate loss (kept behind a flag)
testing_strategy: Fixture inserts 250k distinct URLs and asserts cache.size <= 200000 (side-effect: bound enforced) and that getMeta returns correct metadata (output). Hot-key test: access 'hot' interleaved with churn and assert it survives. Chaos test: simulate extractExif throwing for some URLs and assert failures are not cached as poison entries and do not occupy capacity, and that metrics counters still advance correctly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A periodic full flush evicts hot entries along with cold ones, so immediately after each flush the service re-extracts EXIF for the top images simultaneously, spiking CPU and latency; and between flushes nothing bounds growth, so a flush interval long enough to keep hit rate up still lets the heap reach OOM.

title: Add a CDN cache-key Vary and surrogate keys to fix cross-tenant cache poisoning in a multi-tenant SaaS
business_context: A multi-tenant analytics SaaS serves dashboards from a shared CDN. The CDN cache key ignores the tenant header, so a cached response for tenant A was served to tenant B — a data-leak incident. Engineering disabled CDN caching as an emergency stop, which raised origin load 9x and p95 from 120ms to 1.1s.
constraints: Tenant isolation is mandatory: no cross-tenant response may be served
Must re-enable CDN caching to bring origin load down
Per-tenant purge must be possible without flushing the whole CDN
Auth: tenant derived from a signed JWT claim, not a spoofable header
Backward compatible URLs (cannot put tenant in the path)
p95 target back under 200ms

before_state.description: The CDN caches by URL only and the tenant is read from an unsigned header. Identical URLs across tenants collide on one cache entry, leaking data; caching was disabled as a stopgap.

issues: Cache key ignores tenant -> cross-tenant poisoning
Tenant read from a spoofable header (when it was read at all)
No surrogate keys for per-tenant purge

reasoning: Naive approach: add Vary: X-Tenant. Rejected because the tenant header is client-controlled and spoofable, and Vary on a high-cardinality header that clients can set arbitrarily both leaks (if forgeable) and fragments the cache. Alternative: put the tenant in the URL path — rejected because it breaks existing bookmarked/shared URLs and SEO. Chosen path: derive the tenant from the verified JWT claim at the edge, fold it into a custom cache key (not just Vary), and tag each cached object with a surrogate key per tenant for targeted purge. The edge verifies the JWT, extracts tenantId, and builds a cache key of URL+tenantId so entries are isolated; surrogate-key headers enable per-tenant invalidation. Cross-layer: request hits edge -> JWT verified, tenantId extracted -> cache key = url+tenantId -> origin response tagged Surrogate-Key: tenant-<id> -> purge API targets tenant-<id> -> tests assert tenant A never sees tenant B and that a tenant purge clears only that tenant.

after_state.description: The edge verifies the JWT, derives tenantId, and builds a tenant-scoped cache key so responses can never collide across tenants. Surrogate keys enable per-tenant purge without a global flush.

files.purpose: Verifies JWT, scopes the cache key by tenant, and tags responses for per-tenant purge
Cryptographically verifies the tenant claim so it cannot be spoofed
Targeted purge of one tenant's cached objects via surrogate key
Asserts tenant isolation and that unsigned tenant claims are rejected

migration_strategy: Deploy edge worker with caching still bypassed (CDN_CACHE=off) to validate JWT verification
Enable tenant-scoped caching for internal test tenants only
Run an isolation audit: replay two tenants against the same URL and confirm distinct responses
Enable CDN caching fleet-wide; watch origin load drop and p95 recover
Wire per-tenant purge into the existing invalidation tooling
Rollback: set CDN_CACHE=off to revert to origin-only while keeping JWT verification
testing_strategy: Fixture issues two requests for the same URL with valid JWTs for tenant A and B; assert each gets its own tenant's body (output) and that two distinct cache entries exist (side-effect). Auth test: a request with a forged X-Tenant header and no valid JWT returns 401 and is never cached. Chaos/negative test: an expired JWT bypasses the cache safely (401) and a purge of tenant-A leaves tenant-B's cache entry intact.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Vary: X-Tenant trusts a client-settable header, so an attacker can forge it to read another tenant's cached response, and even when honest it fragments the cache by an unbounded header; neither restores isolation safely, which is why caching had to be disabled in the first place.

title: Switch from full-document caching to field-level caching in MongoDB API to cut serialization cost
business_context: A content API caches entire 400KB article documents in Redis even though most endpoints need only a 2KB summary projection. Every cache hit deserializes 400KB, and writes re-cache the whole blob. At 6k req/s the JSON.parse cost alone consumes 35% of CPU and Redis bandwidth is saturated at 900Mbps.
constraints: List endpoints need only summary fields; detail endpoint needs full body
Cache must not double-store the same data wastefully
Invalidation on edit must cover both summary and full views
Backward compatible response shapes for both endpoints
Redis egress must drop below 400Mbps
No N+1 when listing 50 articles

before_state.description: One cache entry stores the entire document. List endpoints fetch and deserialize the full 400KB blob just to project a 2KB summary, wasting CPU and Redis bandwidth.

issues: Full 400KB doc deserialized even when only summary fields are needed
listSummaries pulls full bodies for 50 articles
High JSON.parse CPU and Redis egress

reasoning: Naive approach: just gzip the cached blob. Rejected because compression reduces egress but the CPU still pays to decompress and parse 400KB to extract a 2KB summary, so the dominant cost remains. Alternative: cache the projected summary inside the list endpoint only — rejected as incomplete because the detail endpoint still re-parses the whole doc and edits would need to invalidate two unrelated representations inconsistently. Chosen path: field-level (split) caching with a shared version. Store two entries per article — a small summary entry and a full-body entry — both stamped with the same document version so a single version bump invalidates both. List endpoints MGET only summary entries; the detail endpoint reads the full entry. Cross-layer: list route -> MGET art:sum:id:ver for all ids (one round trip, small payloads) -> detail route -> GET art:full:id:ver -> edit bumps art:ver -> both representations orphan together -> tests assert list never fetches full bodies and edits invalidate both.

after_state.description: Summary and full-body representations are cached separately under a shared version. List endpoints fetch only small summary entries via MGET; detail fetches the full entry. A single version bump invalidates both consistently.

files.purpose: Splits cache into small summary and full-body entries; list reads only summaries via MGET with a projected DB query
Bumps the shared version so both representations invalidate together on edit
Asserts list avoids full-body reads and that an edit bumps the shared version

migration_strategy: Introduce split keys + version under SPLIT_CACHE=false (still serving full-doc cache)
Dual-write summary and full entries while shadow-reading to compare results
Enable SPLIT_CACHE for the list endpoint first; watch Redis egress drop
Enable for the detail endpoint and confirm parity
Remove the legacy full-doc art:id key and full-doc reads
Rollback: SPLIT_CACHE=false reverts to the single-blob cache
testing_strategy: Fixture stubs mongo find to track projection vs full reads. listSummaries asserts correct summary output AND zero full-body finds with exactly one projected query (side-effects). Edit test: saveArticle bumps the version, invalidating both views. Chaos test: corrupt the summary entry (simulate partial write) and assert listSummaries falls back to a projected DB read for that id and rewrites the summary, while never reading the full body.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Gzipping the blob lowers Redis egress but every list hit still decompresses and JSON.parses 400KB to extract 2KB, so the 35% CPU spent on deserialization persists; the dominant cost is the parse, not the bytes on the wire.

title: Replace synchronous cache writes with async write-behind in a Kafka-fed IoT telemetry cache
business_context: An IoT platform caches the latest reading per device for a real-time map. The Kafka consumer writes every reading synchronously to Redis before committing the offset. At 120k messages/s the consumer is bottlenecked on Redis round-trips, lag grows to 40s, and the live map falls badly behind during traffic spikes.
constraints: Only the latest reading per device matters (older ones are coalesced)
Consumer must keep up with 120k msg/s sustained
Bounded data loss window acceptable (<=1s) on crash, but no unbounded loss
Redis writes should be batched, not one-per-message
At-least-once Kafka semantics preserved for offset commit
Map read API getLatest(deviceId) unchanged

before_state.description: Each Kafka message triggers a synchronous Redis SET before the offset is committed. Per-message round-trips cap throughput far below the message rate, so consumer lag grows.

issues: One synchronous Redis SET per message caps throughput
No coalescing: many readings for one device each cause a write
Consumer lag grows under spikes

reasoning: Naive approach: pipeline a fixed number of SETs. Rejected because pipelining helps but still writes every reading including superseded ones, wasting most writes since only the latest per device matters. Alternative: drop Redis and read latest from a Kafka compacted topic via a key-value store API — rejected as too large a re-architecture for the immediate lag crisis. Chosen path: write-behind with per-device coalescing. The consumer updates an in-memory map of latest-per-device and commits offsets quickly; a flusher periodically writes the coalesced map to Redis with one pipelined MSET. This caps Redis ops at one batch per flush interval regardless of message rate and naturally drops superseded readings. Cross-layer: Kafka message -> update in-memory latest map (cheap) -> commit offset -> flusher every 1s MSETs coalesced batch to Redis -> getLatest reads Redis -> on crash at most the last <=1s of un-flushed updates re-process via at-least-once -> tests assert batched writes and coalescing.

after_state.description: The consumer coalesces the latest reading per device in memory and commits offsets fast; a periodic flusher writes the whole batch with one pipelined MSET, decoupling Redis throughput from message rate.

files.purpose: Hot path only updates the in-memory latest map, removing per-message Redis round-trips
Coalesces and batch-writes latest-per-device to Redis once per second with re-staging on failure
Reads serve the freshest value, preferring the not-yet-flushed in-memory reading
Asserts coalescing reduces N readings to one write per device per flush

migration_strategy: Deploy write-behind with flush interval 250ms and WRITE_BEHIND=false (still sync writes)
Enable on a canary partition; verify consumer lag drops and Redis ops/sec fall
Tune flush interval to balance lag (<1s) and Redis batch size
Ramp to all partitions; confirm 120k msg/s keeps up
Add a metric for buffer size and flush failures
Rollback: WRITE_BEHIND=false reverts to synchronous per-message writes
testing_strategy: Fixture pushes multiple readings for the same device then flushes once; assert the device's Redis value is the latest (output) and that only one SET per device occurred (side-effect of coalescing). Read test: getLatest returns the in-memory value before a flush. Chaos test: make pipeline.exec reject and assert the entries are re-staged into the buffer (no data loss) and the next flush succeeds, keeping the loss window bounded.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain pipelining still issues a write for every message including readings that are immediately superseded, so at 120k msg/s with many readings per device the consumer still does far more Redis work than necessary and lag persists; only coalescing collapses the redundant writes.

title: Fix a hot-key bottleneck on a single Redis shard for a viral post counter using local aggregation
business_context: A social app stores per-post view counters in Redis via INCR. When a post goes viral, all INCRs for that post route to one shard (single key), pushing that shard to 100% CPU at ~80k INCR/s while other shards idle. The counter falls behind and the displayed view count visibly stalls during the viral spike.
constraints: Counts can be eventually consistent (a few seconds of lag acceptable)
Must not lose increments (no dropped views)
Single hot key must not saturate one shard
Read API getViews(postId) returns a total
Solution must work across 30 API instances
No change to how clients report a view

before_state.description: Every view does INCR on one key per post. A viral post concentrates all traffic on a single key and thus one shard, which saturates while the rest of the cluster is idle.

issues: All increments for a viral post hit one key/shard -> CPU saturation
No sharding of the counter across slots
Per-view network round-trip under extreme rate

reasoning: Naive approach: move the hot key to its own dedicated shard. Rejected because it just relocates the bottleneck; a single key still funnels all writes to one CPU and the next viral post recreates the problem. Alternative: switch reads/writes to a CRDT counter in a different datastore — rejected as too heavy for an urgent fix and it changes the read path semantics. Chosen path: counter sharding plus per-instance local aggregation. Each API instance buffers increments locally and flushes them as larger ADDs to one of N sub-counter keys (views:postId:bucket) chosen so they hash to different slots; reads sum the N buckets. Local aggregation cuts the op rate (one flush per interval instead of per view) and bucket fan-out spreads load across shards. Cross-layer: recordView increments a local in-memory counter -> flusher every 500ms INCRBY to views:postId:{instanceBucket} -> buckets spread across slots -> getViews sums the buckets -> tests assert no increment lost and load spread across multiple keys.

after_state.description: Increments are aggregated per-instance and flushed as INCRBY to one of several bucket keys that hash to different slots, spreading the hot post's load across the cluster. Reads sum the buckets.

files.purpose: Local aggregation plus bucketed keys spread a hot counter across shards without losing increments

files.content:
import { startFlusher } from './counter';
startFlusher(); // begin per-instance flush loop at boot
files.purpose: Starts the per-instance flusher so buffered increments are persisted
Asserts no lost increments and that load spreads across multiple keys/slots

migration_strategy: Deploy bucketed counter with reads that sum both legacy single key and buckets
Enable local aggregation writes to buckets behind BUCKET_COUNTERS=false initially (still single-key INCR)
Turn on bucketed writes on canary; verify shard CPU spreads and no count regressions
Backfill legacy single-key value into bucket 0 once, then ramp to 100%
Stop reading the legacy single key after backfill verified
Rollback: BUCKET_COUNTERS=false reverts to single-key INCR (reads still sum buckets, so no data loss)
testing_strategy: Fixture records 1000 views for one post, flushes, and asserts getViews === 1000 (output: no lost increments). Spread test: simulate multiple instance buckets flushing and assert more than one bucket key is written (side-effect: load spread). Chaos test: make a flush exec reject mid-batch and assert the increments are re-staged locally and counted after the next successful flush, so the final total is still exact.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Moving the hot key to a dedicated shard only relocates the single-key bottleneck: all 80k INCR/s for the viral post still hammer one CPU core, so that shard saturates again and the next viral post on a different key recreates the identical hotspot.

title: Add cache versioning to safely change a cached object schema without poisoning old readers
business_context: A Vue/Nuxt storefront caches a serialized cart-pricing object in Redis. A deploy changed the object shape (renamed a field, added a nested discounts array). Old app instances during the rolling deploy read the new-shaped cached object and crashed on the missing old field, causing a 7-minute partial outage where ~15% of carts failed to render.
constraints: Schema changes must not break instances still running the old code during a rolling deploy
No full cache flush (would stampede the pricing engine)
Old and new readers coexist for the deploy window (up to 20 min)
Cache entries must be self-describing about their schema
Existing getCartPricing(cartId) signature unchanged
Corrupt/incompatible entries must be treated as a miss, not a crash

before_state.description: Cached objects carry no schema version. After a shape change, mixed old/new instances deserialize each other's entries and crash on missing or renamed fields.

issues: No schema version on cached objects
Old code crashes reading new-shaped entries (and vice versa)
No safe fallback to treat incompatible entries as a miss

reasoning: Naive approach: flush all cart keys on deploy so only new-shaped entries exist. Rejected because flushing stampedes the pricing engine (every active cart recomputes at once) and old instances still write old-shaped entries during the rolling window, re-poisoning the cache. Alternative: dual-read both shapes with try/catch — rejected as brittle and not self-describing, leading to silent data confusion. Chosen path: embed a schema version in the cache namespace and in the payload, and validate on read. Each code version reads/writes its own schema-versioned key (cart:v3:id), so old and new instances never read each other's entries; an unrecognized or invalid entry is treated as a miss and recomputed. Cross-layer: getCartPricing keys by SCHEMA_VERSION -> reads only its own version's entries -> writes stamped with the version -> mismatched/old entries are simply not found by the new key and TTL out -> a validator guards against corrupt payloads -> tests assert old and new versions are isolated and bad entries become misses.

after_state.description: Cache keys and payloads carry an explicit schema version, so each code version only reads compatible entries. Incompatible or corrupt entries are treated as misses and recomputed, never crashing a reader.

files.purpose: Schema-versioned keys and payloads isolate readers and turn incompatible entries into safe misses
Centralizes the schema version and a structural validator used as a read guard
Asserts version isolation and that corrupt entries never crash the reader

migration_strategy: Bump SCHEMA_VERSION in the new build so it reads/writes cart:v3 keys
Deploy as a rolling update; old instances keep using cart:v2 keys untouched
During the window both versions populate their own namespaces, no cross-reads
After the deploy completes, let cart:v2 keys TTL out (no flush needed)
Add the validate() guard so any future corrupt entry degrades to a recompute
Rollback: redeploy old build; it resumes reading cart:v2 entries that are still valid for it
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds an old-version entry (cart:v2) and asserts the new code computes fresh (output) because it only looks up cart:v3 (side-effect: one compute, old entry untouched). Corrupt-entry test: seed invalid JSON under the current version and assert getCartPricing recomputes instead of throwing. Chaos test: simulate a rolling deploy by interleaving old-build and new-build reads/writes and assert neither version ever deserializes the other's payload.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Flushing on deploy triggers a full recompute stampede on the pricing engine, and because old instances keep writing old-shaped entries during the rolling window, the new instances would still encounter incompatible objects — so the crash returns until every old instance is gone.

title: Add circuit-breaker fallback so a Redis outage degrades to DB instead of taking down the API
business_context: A fintech account-balance API depends on a Redis cache. During a Redis maintenance event, every request blocked on Redis connection timeouts (default 10s), exhausting the Node thread/connection pool; the entire API returned 503s even though Postgres was healthy. The 11-minute Redis blip became a 25-minute full API outage.
constraints: Redis must be a performance optimization, never a hard dependency
A Redis outage must degrade to DB reads, not fail the request
Connection attempts must fail fast (no 10s hangs)
Avoid hammering a recovering Redis (half-open probing)
Existing getBalance(accountId) signature unchanged
DB must not be overwhelmed if the breaker opens (bounded fallback load)

before_state.description: The cache call has a long timeout and no breaker. When Redis is unavailable, every request blocks for the full timeout and the pool is exhausted, turning a cache outage into a total API outage.

issues: No timeout/breaker: Redis outage blocks every request for 10s
Pool exhaustion turns a cache blip into a full outage
No DB fallback path

reasoning: Naive approach: lower the Redis socket timeout to 200ms. Rejected as insufficient alone because under a sustained outage every request still pays 200ms and attempts a doomed connection, multiplying failed connects and keeping the pool pressured. Alternative: wrap every call in try/catch and fall to DB — rejected as the sole fix because it still attempts Redis on every request during the outage, and an unbounded DB fallback can overwhelm Postgres. Chosen path: a circuit breaker with fast timeouts plus DB fallback. Failures trip the breaker open; while open, calls skip Redis entirely and go straight to DB (fast, no doomed connects); a half-open state periodically probes recovery. Cross-layer: getBalance asks the breaker -> closed: short-timeout Redis read with DB fallback on error -> open: bypass Redis, read DB directly -> half-open: single probe to test Redis -> DB fallback bounded by the existing query path -> tests assert open-breaker requests never touch Redis and succeed via DB.

after_state.description: A circuit breaker wraps Redis with fast timeouts. On repeated failures it opens and bypasses Redis to read the DB directly, half-opens to probe recovery, and closes when Redis is healthy — so a Redis outage degrades gracefully.

files.purpose: Routes around Redis when the breaker is open and falls back to DB on any failure
Circuit breaker with fast timeout, open/half-open/closed states bounding doomed Redis calls
Asserts breaker opens on failures, bypasses Redis, and recovers via half-open

migration_strategy: Deploy breaker with thresholds tuned conservatively and BREAKER=false (legacy direct calls)
Enable BREAKER on canary; run a Redis chaos test (kill Redis) and confirm requests succeed via DB
Verify fast-fail timing (no 10s hangs) under simulated outage
Ramp to fleet; add alert on breaker open events
Tune openMs and timeoutMs from observed recovery behavior
Rollback: BREAKER=false restores direct Redis calls
testing_strategy: Fixture forces Redis to reject; assert getBalance returns the correct DB balance (output) and that after the failure threshold the breaker is open so subsequent calls do not invoke redis.get at all (side-effect: zero Redis calls while open). Recovery test: half-open probe succeeds and the breaker closes. Chaos test: simulate Redis flapping (fail, recover, fail) and assert no request ever exceeds the configured timeout and none return a 503.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Just lowering the socket timeout means every request during a sustained outage still attempts and waits on a doomed Redis connect; the repeated failed connects keep the pool under pressure and add latency to every request, so the API still degrades badly even if it no longer fully hangs.

title: Pre-warm cold caches on deploy to avoid a post-deploy latency cliff in a Django checkout service
business_context: An e-commerce checkout service caches shipping-rate tables in Redis. Every blue/green deploy spins up fresh instances with cold local caches; the first wave of checkout requests after cutover all miss and recompute rate tables (450ms each), spiking checkout p95 to 1.9s for ~3 minutes after every deploy. The team deploys 6 times/day, so this hurts conversion daily.
constraints: Rate tables are large (~5MB) and expensive to compute (450ms)
New instances must serve at full speed at cutover, not after warming organically
Warm-up must not itself stampede the rate engine
Deploys happen 6x/day and must not slow down
Existing get_rates(zone) API unchanged
Shared Redis is the warm source of truth between deploys

before_state.description: Each instance has a local in-process cache and falls back to Redis then the rate engine. Fresh instances start cold, so the first requests after cutover all miss and recompute, causing a latency cliff.

issues: Fresh instances start with an empty _local cache -> first requests all miss
No warm-up before the instance receives production traffic
Cutover causes a synchronized miss wave

reasoning: Naive approach: increase the local cache TTL. Rejected because TTL does nothing for a brand-new instance whose local cache is empty at boot; the cliff is about cold start, not expiry. Alternative: skip the local cache and always hit Redis — rejected because the per-request Redis round-trip for a 5MB table adds latency and bandwidth at checkout's hottest path. Chosen path: an explicit warm-up step gated by the readiness probe. On boot, the instance pre-loads the top-N hot zones from Redis (already warm from prior deploys) into its local cache before reporting ready; the load balancer only routes traffic once warm. If a zone is missing in Redis, a single guarded recompute fills it without stampeding. Cross-layer: instance boots -> warmup loads hot zones from Redis into _local -> readiness probe returns 200 only after warmup -> LB routes traffic to a hot instance -> get_rates serves from _local with no cliff -> tests assert readiness gates on warm-up and that warm-up uses Redis, not mass recompute.

after_state.description: On boot, instances pre-load hot zones from the shared (already warm) Redis into the local cache and only report ready after warm-up, so they serve at full speed at cutover with no synchronized miss wave.

files.purpose: Pre-loads hot zones from shared Redis into the local cache and tracks readiness
Adds a single-flight guard around recompute so cold zones do not stampede the rate engine
Readiness probe returns 503 until warm-up completes so the LB withholds traffic until hot

files.content:
def test_readiness_gates_on_warmup(clean):
    seed_redis_rates(['z1','z2'])
    assert is_ready() is False
    warm()
    assert is_ready() is True
    assert 'z1' in _local and 'z2' in _local

def test_warmup_uses_redis_not_recompute(clean):
    seed_redis_rates(['z1'])
    calls = patch_compute_counter()
    warm()
    assert calls() == 0  # warm-up read Redis, did not recompute
files.purpose: Asserts readiness gates on warm-up and that warm-up avoids mass recompute

migration_strategy: Add warm() and the readiness probe; deploy with the LB still using the old liveness probe
Point the LB readiness check at the new endpoint in staging and verify traffic waits for warm
Canary one instance: confirm zero post-cutover miss wave in traces
Roll warm-up to all instances; measure checkout p95 stays flat through a deploy
Tune HOT_ZONES from traffic data to cover the warm set
Rollback: revert the readiness probe to liveness-only (instances serve immediately, accepting the cliff)
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds Redis with hot-zone rate tables. Assert is_ready() is False before warm() and True after, with the hot zones present in _local (output + side-effect). Anti-stampede test: with a zone present in Redis, assert warm() makes zero rate_engine.compute calls. Chaos test: remove one hot zone from Redis so warm-up cannot load it, then assert the first request for that zone recomputes under the single-flight lock (only one compute) and readiness is not blocked by the single missing zone.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Raising the local TTL changes nothing at cold start because a freshly booted instance has an empty local cache; at cutover the load balancer immediately sends full traffic to instances that all miss and recompute the 450ms rate tables simultaneously, producing the post-deploy latency cliff every single deploy.

title: Fix lock-based stampede protection that deadlocks when the lock holder crashes mid-recompute
business_context: A devtools service caches expensive dependency-graph computations. It uses a Redis SETNX lock so one worker recomputes while others wait. But the lock has no TTL safety and waiters poll indefinitely; when a lock-holding worker OOM-crashed mid-recompute, the lock was never released, every waiter spun forever, and the endpoint hung until a manual Redis DEL. This caused a 22-minute outage.
constraints: Lock must auto-expire so a crashed holder cannot deadlock the system
A worker must only release a lock it actually owns (no releasing someone else's lock)
Waiters must time out and degrade rather than spin forever
Recompute can exceed the initial lock TTL (needs lock extension)
Existing getGraph(repoId) signature unchanged
Only one recompute per repo at a time

before_state.description: A SETNX lock with no TTL and indefinite waiter polling. A crash by the lock holder leaves the lock held forever and waiters spin, deadlocking the endpoint.

issues: Lock has no TTL: a crashed holder deadlocks forever
Waiters spin indefinitely with no timeout
del releases regardless of ownership (could delete a renewed lock)

reasoning: Naive approach: add a fixed TTL to the lock. Rejected as insufficient because if the recompute legitimately runs longer than the TTL, the lock expires mid-work, a second worker acquires it, and the first worker's later DEL releases the second worker's lock — a classic double-release bug.
