Alternative: use a distributed lock library blindly — acceptable but we still need correct ownership and bounded waiting. Chosen path: a fenced lock with a unique token, a watchdog that extends the TTL while work continues, ownership-checked release via a Lua compare-and-delete, and bounded waiter timeouts that degrade to serving stale or recomputing without the lock. Cross-layer: getGraph SET NX PX with a random token -> watchdog extends TTL via PEXPIRE while computing -> release runs a Lua script that deletes only if token matches -> waiters poll with a deadline then fall back -> tests assert a crashed holder's lock expires and that release never deletes a non-owned lock.

after_state.description: The lock uses a unique token with a TTL, a watchdog extends it during long recomputes, and release is an ownership-checked Lua compare-and-delete. Waiters time out and degrade instead of spinning forever.

files.purpose: Fenced lock with token-checked release and a TTL-extend primitive
Bounded waiting with a watchdog-extended, ownership-safe lock and a degrade-to-recompute path
Asserts auto-expiry prevents deadlock and that release respects ownership

migration_strategy: Deploy fenced lock + watchdog behind FENCED_LOCK=false (legacy SETNX path)
Enable on canary; run a chaos test killing the lock holder mid-compute and confirm recovery
Verify waiter deadline degrades to recompute instead of spinning
Ramp to 100%; remove the legacy SETNX branch
Add alerts on watchdog-extend failures and lock acquisition timeouts
Rollback: FENCED_LOCK=false reverts to the old lock (with the known risk) if needed
testing_strategy: Fixture acquires a lock with a short TTL and never releases (simulating a crash), then asserts the lock becomes re-acquirable after expiry (side-effect: no deadlock). Ownership test: release with a wrong token leaves the lock intact. Chaos test: start a recompute, kill the holder mid-flight, and assert waiters within the deadline either get a value from a new holder or degrade to their own recompute, and that the endpoint never hangs past the 8s deadline.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A fixed-TTL lock without a watchdog or ownership check breaks when the recompute outlives the TTL: the lock expires, a second worker acquires it, and the first worker's eventual DEL deletes the second worker's lock, allowing a third worker in — so two recomputes run concurrently and the stampede protection silently fails.

title: Compress and pack a large hash-of-sessions cache to fix Redis memory pressure in a B2B SaaS
business_context: A B2B SaaS stores each user session as a separate Redis string key with a 1KB JSON blob. With 12M active sessions, key overhead plus JSON bloat pushes Redis to 22GB and rising; the team faces a forced vertical scale-up costing $1,800/mo, and large-key fragmentation is causing latency jitter.
constraints: Session reads/writes must stay sub-millisecond
Must reduce Redis memory by at least 40%
Per-session TTL/expiry behavior must be preserved
Backward compatible getSession/setSession API
No external store change (stay on Redis)
Must not break horizontal sharding (key distribution)

before_state.description: Each session is a standalone string key holding verbose JSON. Per-key overhead and JSON verbosity dominate memory at 12M keys, and large values fragment the allocator.

issues: 12M standalone keys -> high per-key overhead
Verbose JSON (~1KB) inflates each value
Large values fragment the allocator, causing latency jitter

reasoning: Naive approach: just set a shorter TTL to keep fewer sessions. Rejected because it degrades UX (users logged out sooner) and does not address per-key overhead or JSON bloat for the sessions that remain. Alternative: switch every value to MsgPack only — rejected as insufficient alone because the dominant waste is per-key overhead at 12M keys, not just payload encoding. Chosen path: pack sessions into Redis hashes bucketed by a hash of the session id (e.g. sessions are stored as fields in one of 4096 hash buckets) and serialize values with MsgPack. Hash packing amortizes per-key overhead across many sessions; MsgPack shrinks the payload; ziplist/listpack-encoded small hashes are memory-dense. Cross-layer: setSession computes bucket = hash(sid) % 4096 -> HSET bucket sid msgpack(data) -> getSession HGET bucket sid -> per-session TTL handled via a companion expiry field swept by a janitor (or hash-field TTL where available) -> sharding still works since buckets distribute -> tests assert memory drop and correct round-trip.

after_state.description: Sessions are packed into bucketed Redis hashes with MsgPack-encoded values, amortizing per-key overhead and shrinking payloads. A lightweight expiry field plus a janitor preserves per-session TTL semantics.

files.purpose: Packs sessions into bucketed hashes with MsgPack and an embedded expiry to cut memory
Periodically removes expired session fields to preserve TTL semantics and reclaim memory
Asserts correct round-trip and expiry behavior under packed storage

migration_strategy: Deploy packed store with PACK_SESSIONS=false; reads check packed hash then fall back to legacy sess:sid
Dual-write new sessions to packed hashes while still reading legacy on miss
Migrate existing sessions lazily on access (read legacy, write packed, delete legacy)
Enable PACK_SESSIONS reads-first on canary; verify memory drop and latency
Run the janitor; once legacy keys drained, remove the fallback
Rollback: PACK_SESSIONS=false reverts to standalone keys (dual-written data still readable)
testing_strategy: Fixture writes and reads a session through the packed store; assert the data round-trips correctly (output) and that it is stored as a hash field, not a standalone key (side-effect, via key-type inspection). Expiry test: a session with a past _exp returns null and is removed by the janitor. Memory test: load 100k sessions in packed vs legacy form and assert packed uses >=40% less Redis memory (used_memory delta).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Shortening TTLs evicts sessions sooner, harming UX, but does nothing about the structural cost: 12M standalone keys still carry large per-key overhead and verbose JSON, so memory stays high for the sessions that remain and the allocator keeps fragmenting on the large values.

title: Coordinate multi-region cache invalidation so an edit propagates to all PoPs within SLA
business_context: A global SaaS runs Redis caches in 5 regions (us, eu, ap, sa, me). A config change in the primary region only invalidated the local Redis; other regions kept serving stale config for up to the 10-minute TTL. A customer in eu saw a disabled feature stay enabled for 9 minutes after they toggled it off, breaching the product's 30-second propagation promise.
constraints: Invalidation must reach all 5 regions within 30 seconds
Regional Redis instances are independent (no cross-region Redis replication for cache)
Must be resilient to one region's invalidation message failing
No global write lock across regions
Existing getConfig(orgId) read path unchanged
Idempotent invalidation (duplicate messages are safe)

before_state.description: Config writes invalidate only the local region's Redis. Other regions have no signal and keep serving stale config until the TTL expires, far beyond the 30s SLA.

issues: Only the local region's cache is invalidated on write
Other regions serve stale config until TTL
No cross-region propagation mechanism

reasoning: Naive approach: lower the TTL to 30s so staleness is bounded. Rejected because a 30s TTL across 5 regions multiplies DB read load (every region re-reads config 20x more often) and still allows up to 30s of staleness on top of the write — and it does nothing for an explicit edit needing immediate effect. Alternative: cross-region Redis active-active replication for the cache — rejected as overkill and expensive for invalidation-only signals and it couples regional failure domains. Chosen path: publish invalidation events to a global pub/sub backbone (e.g. a Kafka or SNS topic) that every region subscribes to. On updateConfig, the primary writes the DB and publishes an org-scoped invalidation; each region's subscriber deletes its local key. Messages are idempotent (delete is idempotent) and a region that misses one is bounded by the TTL as a backstop. Cross-layer: updateConfig writes DB then publishes invalidation -> global topic fans out to all regions -> each regional subscriber deletes cfg:orgId locally -> next read repopulates from DB -> TTL remains as a safety backstop -> tests assert all regions invalidate within SLA and that a dropped message degrades gracefully.

after_state.description: Config edits publish an idempotent invalidation event to a global topic; every region's subscriber deletes its local cache key, achieving sub-30s propagation with the TTL as a backstop for missed messages.

files.purpose: Write path invalidates locally and publishes a global invalidation event
Global fan-out producer and an idempotent per-region subscriber that deletes local keys
Asserts an edit clears the cache in every region via the bus

migration_strategy: Stand up the global invalidation topic and deploy regional subscribers (consuming, not yet authoritative)
Enable publishInvalidation on writes behind GLOBAL_INVALIDATION=false (local del only)
Turn on global publishing in one region; verify other regions delete within seconds
Measure end-to-end propagation against the 30s SLA
Enable fleet-wide; keep the TTL backstop at 600s for missed-message safety
Rollback: GLOBAL_INVALIDATION=false reverts to local-only invalidation
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds three regional Redis instances with a stale config and starts subscribers. Call updateConfig and drain the bus; assert every region's key is null (side-effect across regions) and that a re-read returns the new DB config (output). Idempotency test: deliver the same invalidation twice and assert no error and the key stays absent. Chaos test: drop the message to one region and assert that region still self-heals within the TTL backstop while the others propagate within 30s.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Local-only invalidation never signals the other four regions, so they keep serving the cached config until their independent 10-minute TTL expires; lowering the TTL only shrinks the staleness window at the cost of multiplying cross-region DB load and still cannot make an explicit edit take effect immediately.

title: Stop caching personalized responses in a shared cache that leaked user-specific data
business_context: A news app's API cached the /feed response in a shared Redis without including the user in the key. Because feeds are personalized (followed topics, hidden sources), user A's cached feed was served to user B — a privacy incident affecting an estimated 4,000 users over 40 minutes before it was caught. The quick mitigation disabled feed caching, raising DB load 6x.
constraints: Personalized content must never be served to the wrong user
Must restore caching to bring DB load down
Common (non-personalized) feed segments should still be shareable to keep hit rate up
User identity must come from the authenticated session, not a header
Existing getFeed(req) entry point unchanged
Cache key must be deterministic per (user, feed-params)

before_state.description: The feed cache key omits the user identity, so a personalized response is shared across users. The mitigation was to disable caching entirely, which overloaded the DB.

issues: Key omits userId -> personalized feed leaks to other users
No separation of shared vs personalized content
Mitigation (disable cache) overloaded the DB

reasoning: Naive approach: add userId to the key. This fixes the leak but rejected as the complete solution because per-user keys collapse hit rate to near zero (each user is unique), keeping DB load high — the very problem caching was meant to solve. Alternative: never cache the feed — rejected, that is the current overloaded mitigation. Chosen path: split the response into a shared base layer (cacheable across users: the candidate article set for the topic/page) and a thin personalization layer applied per request (ranking, hidden sources, read state). The shared base is cached with a non-user key and reused across users; personalization is computed cheaply on top per user and never cached in the shared store. Cross-layer: getFeed derives userId from the session -> fetch shared base from cache (high hit rate) -> apply per-user personalization in memory -> return -> shared cache never contains user-specific data -> tests assert two users get different final feeds from the same cached base and that the cache holds no PII.

after_state.description: The cacheable shared base (topic candidate set) is cached without user identity and reused across users; personalization is applied per request on top and never cached, restoring hit rate while eliminating the leak.

files.purpose: Caches only the user-independent candidate set and personalizes per request
Applies user-specific filtering and ranking without ever writing user data to the shared cache
Asserts cross-user reuse of the base, distinct personalized outputs, and no PII in the shared cache

migration_strategy: Introduce getSharedBase + personalize behind SPLIT_FEED=false (still disabled-cache mitigation)
Enable shared-base caching for a non-personalized topic to validate the base layer
Audit the shared cache contents to confirm zero user-specific fields
Enable SPLIT_FEED on canary; verify two test users get distinct feeds from one base
Ramp to 100%; DB load drops as base hit rate rises
Rollback: SPLIT_FEED=false reverts to the safe (but slow) no-cache mitigation
testing_strategy: Fixture builds the candidate set with a counter and requests the same topic/page for two different users. Assert the base is built once (side-effect: cross-user reuse) and the two final feeds differ (output: personalization applied). PII test: inspect the shared cache value and assert it contains no user identifiers. Chaos test: corrupt a user's prefs lookup and assert personalize degrades to the unfiltered base for that user without leaking another user's preferences.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding userId to the single cache key stops the leak but makes every key unique per user, so hit rate collapses to roughly zero and the DB load that triggered the incident's emergency cache-disable returns in full; the cache no longer offloads anything.

title: Add ETag/Last-Modified conditional caching to a REST API to cut redundant payload transfer
business_context: A mobile API returns a 250KB product-catalog JSON on every poll. Clients poll every 30s for changes that occur a few times a day, so 99% of responses are identical full payloads. This wastes ~40TB/month of egress and drains mobile battery/data; the catalog rarely changes but clients always download the whole thing.
constraints: Clients must get a 304 Not Modified when the catalog is unchanged
ETag must change only when the catalog content actually changes
Server-side computation of the response must itself be cached (not recomputed per poll)
Backward compatible with clients that ignore conditional headers
Egress must drop by at least 90% for unchanged polls
Works behind a CDN that honors conditional requests

before_state.description: Every request rebuilds and returns the full catalog JSON with no validators, so unchanged content is re-serialized and re-transferred on every poll.

issues: No ETag/Last-Modified -> clients always download full payload
buildCatalog recomputed per request
Massive redundant egress for unchanged data

reasoning: Naive approach: gzip the response. Rejected because compression shrinks each transfer but clients still download a full (compressed) body every 30s for unchanged data — the redundant transfers remain. Alternative: switch clients to a delta/sync protocol — rejected as too invasive for existing mobile clients and a much larger change. Chosen path: HTTP conditional caching with a content-derived ETag plus a server-side response cache. Compute a strong ETag from a hash of the catalog content; cache the serialized body and its ETag in Redis so polls do not recompute; respond 304 when If-None-Match matches, sending an empty body. Cross-layer: client sends If-None-Match -> handler reads cached {etag, body} from Redis -> if client ETag matches, return 304 empty -> else return body + ETag -> ETag recomputed only when buildCatalog output changes (invalidated on catalog write) -> CDN can also serve 304s -> tests assert 304 with no body on match and 200 with body on change.

after_state.description: Responses carry a content-derived ETag; the serialized body and ETag are cached server-side. Unchanged polls return a 304 with no body, and the catalog is only re-serialized when it actually changes.

files.purpose: Serves conditional 304s and a cached serialized body with a content ETag
Caches the serialized body and content-derived ETag so polls do not recompute
Asserts 304 on match (no body) and a fresh 200 with a new ETag after a change

migration_strategy: Deploy ETag + server-side response cache with COND_CACHE=false (still full responses)
Enable on canary; measure 304 ratio and egress reduction for polling clients
Wire catalog write paths to call invalidate() so ETags change on real edits
Ramp to 100% and confirm >=90% egress drop on unchanged polls
Verify CDN honors conditional requests end-to-end
Rollback: COND_CACHE=false reverts to always-200 full responses
testing_strategy: Fixture issues an initial GET to capture the ETag, then a conditional GET with If-None-Match and asserts a 304 with an empty body (output + side-effect: no payload). Change test: mutate the catalog, invalidate, and assert the next conditional request returns 200 with a different ETag. Chaos test: corrupt the cached response entry and assert getCatalogResponse rebuilds it (recomputes body + ETag) rather than serving a malformed 304.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Gzip alone still sends a full body on every poll, so 99% of the 30s polls keep transferring the unchanged catalog (just compressed); the redundant transfers and their egress cost remain because nothing tells the client the content is identical to what it already has.

title: Replace a blocking cache-rebuild with serve-stale-on-error to survive a flaky upstream in a Go API
business_context: A Go gateway caches responses from a flaky partner pricing API. The cache uses a hard TTL; when an entry expires and the partner API is slow or 500ing, the rebuild blocks and fails, so the gateway returns errors to users even though a slightly-stale-but-valid value was just in the cache. During a partner incident, 28% of requests failed for 12 minutes despite usable stale data existing.
constraints: When the upstream fails on rebuild, serve the last good value rather than erroring
Stale data is acceptable up to a bounded grace window (5 minutes)
Beyond the grace window, fail explicitly (do not serve ancient data)
Only one rebuild per key at a time (no stampede on the flaky upstream)
Existing Get(ctx, key) signature unchanged
Must record when stale was served (observability)

before_state.description: Entries have a hard TTL; on expiry the rebuild calls the flaky upstream synchronously and, if it fails, returns an error to the caller even though a recently-valid value existed moments earlier.

issues: Hard TTL: on expiry a failed rebuild returns an error
No last-good fallback even though stale data existed
No single-flight, so a flaky upstream gets hammered by concurrent rebuilds

reasoning: Naive approach: just extend the TTL during incidents. Rejected because TTL is static and cannot know the upstream is failing; a long TTL serves stale even when fresh is available, and a short one fails during incidents — neither adapts. Alternative: retry the upstream a few times on rebuild — rejected because retries against a 500ing partner add latency and load without changing the outcome when the partner is down. Chosen path: a soft-TTL with a stale grace window plus serve-stale-on-error and single-flight. Each entry stores value, freshUntil, and staleUntil. Within freshUntil serve directly; between freshUntil and staleUntil, trigger a single background rebuild but serve the stale value immediately; if rebuild fails, keep serving stale until staleUntil; beyond staleUntil, attempt a blocking rebuild and only then error. Cross-layer: Get checks entry timestamps -> fresh: return -> stale-within-grace: return stale + async single-flight rebuild -> rebuild error: extend stale, record metric -> beyond grace: blocking rebuild, error if it fails -> tests assert stale served during an upstream outage and hard failure only past the grace window.

after_state.description: Entries carry freshUntil and staleUntil. Within the grace window the gateway serves stale instantly while one background rebuild runs; if the upstream fails it keeps serving stale until the grace expires, only erroring beyond it.

files.purpose: Soft-TTL with serve-stale-on-error and single-flight background refresh
Single-flight blocking rebuild and timestamp management for fresh/stale windows
Asserts stale is served during an upstream failure inside the grace window

migration_strategy: Deploy soft-TTL cache with grace=0 so behavior matches the old hard-TTL initially
Enable a small grace window on canary and inject upstream failures to confirm stale is served
Verify single-flight prevents rebuild stampede against the flaky upstream
Ramp grace to 5 minutes fleet-wide; watch error rate drop during partner blips
Add a ServedStale metric and alert if stale-serving is sustained
Rollback: set grace=0 to revert to strict hard-TTL behavior
testing_strategy: Fixture warms a key, advances past freshUntil into the grace window, then makes the upstream fail and asserts Get returns the stale data with no error (output) and increments the ServedStale metric (side-effect). Hard-fail test: advance past staleUntil with a failing upstream and assert Get returns an error (no ancient data served). Chaos test: 1000 concurrent Gets during a stale window with a failing upstream and assert exactly one rebuild attempt (single-flight) and zero user-facing errors.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A static TTL cannot react to upstream health: when the partner API is failing at the moment an entry expires, the synchronous rebuild fails and the error propagates to the user even though a perfectly usable value was cached seconds earlier — so the gateway returns errors despite having good-enough data.

title: Fix a cache that returns serialized DB connection objects causing nondeterministic reads
business_context: A Python analytics service cached query result objects that included lazy SQLAlchemy relationship proxies. Pickling them stored bound session references; on cache hit, accessing a relationship raised DetachedInstanceError or, worse, silently returned empty collections. ~2% of dashboard panels rendered blank intermittently, and the bug was nearly impossible to reproduce locally.
constraints: Cached values must be fully detached, serializable plain data
No ORM session state may leak into the cache
Round-trip must be deterministic across processes
Backward compatible with existing get_report(qid) callers
Cache hit must never trigger a lazy DB load
Must handle nested relationships eagerly resolved

before_state.description: ORM entities (with lazy relationships and bound sessions) are pickled directly into the cache. On a hit in another process the session is gone, so relationship access fails or returns empty.

files.content:
import pickle

def get_report(qid):
    raw = r.get(f'rep:{qid}')
    if raw:
        return pickle.loads(raw)  # ORM objects with dead sessions
    rows = session.query(Report).filter_by(qid=qid).all()  # lazy relationships
    r.set(f'rep:{qid}', pickle.dumps(rows), ex=300)
    return rows
issues: Pickles ORM entities carrying session-bound lazy relationships
On hit, lazy access raises DetachedInstanceError or returns empty
Nondeterministic blank panels, hard to reproduce

reasoning: Naive approach: call expunge_all before pickling. Rejected because detaching does not eagerly load unfetched relationships, so accessing them after deserialization still fails or returns empty; the lazy-load trap remains. Alternative: cache raw SQL rows as tuples — rejected because it loses the typed/nested structure the dashboard expects and pushes mapping logic into every caller. Chosen path: introduce an explicit DTO serialization boundary. Convert ORM entities to plain dataclasses/dicts via a to_dto() that eagerly materializes every needed relationship, cache the DTO as JSON (no pickle, no session), and reconstruct typed DTOs on read. The ORM never enters the cache. Cross-layer: get_report queries with eager joins -> map to DTO (plain data) -> JSON-encode into cache -> read decodes JSON to DTO -> dashboard consumes DTO with no lazy loads -> tests assert a cache hit in a fresh process renders fully populated data.

after_state.description: Entities are mapped to plain JSON-serializable DTOs with all relationships eagerly resolved before caching. Reads reconstruct DTOs without any ORM session, so hits are deterministic and never lazy-load.

files.purpose: Defines a session-free DTO with eager relationship materialization and JSON round-trip

files.content:
import json
from .dto import ReportDTO

def get_report(qid):
    raw = r.get(f'rep:{qid}')
    if raw:
        return ReportDTO.from_json(json.loads(raw))  # pure data, no ORM
    rep = (session.query(Report)
           .options(joinedload(Report.metrics))      # eager load
           .filter_by(qid=qid).one())
    dto = ReportDTO.from_orm(rep)
    r.set(f'rep:{qid}', json.dumps(dto.to_json()), ex=300)
    return dto
files.purpose: Caches DTOs as JSON with eager-loaded relationships, eliminating session leaks

files.content:
def test_hit_in_fresh_process_is_fully_populated(clean):
    seed_report('q1', metrics=[('cpu', 1.0), ('mem', 2.0)])
    get_report('q1')          # populates cache
    session.close()           # simulate a different process/session
    dto = get_report('q1')    # hit
    assert [m.name for m in dto.metrics] == ['cpu', 'mem']

def test_no_lazy_load_on_hit(clean):
    seed_report('q1', metrics=[('cpu', 1.0)])
    get_report('q1')
    with assert_no_db_queries():
        get_report('q1')      # must not touch the DB
files.purpose: Asserts a fresh-session hit is fully populated and triggers no DB queries

migration_strategy: Add DTO types and from_orm/from_json mappers without changing the cache yet
Switch writes to store DTO JSON while reads still accept both pickle and JSON
Backfill: let old pickle entries TTL out as JSON entries replace them
Enable JSON-only reads on canary; verify panels render fully across processes
Remove the pickle read path once no pickle entries remain
Rollback: re-enable dual-read (pickle+JSON) if any mapping gap is found
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds a report with metrics, populates the cache, then closes the session to mimic a separate process and asserts the hit returns fully-populated metrics (output). Side-effect test: wrap the second get_report in an assert-no-DB-queries guard to prove no lazy load occurs. Chaos test: store a legacy pickle entry and assert the dual-read path either decodes it or treats it as a miss and rewrites JSON, never raising DetachedInstanceError.
what_breaks_naive_approach: expunge_all detaches entities but does not eagerly load their relationships, so when the dashboard accesses report.metrics after a cross-process cache hit there is no live session to lazy-load from — SQLAlchemy raises DetachedInstanceError or returns an empty collection, reproducing the intermittent blank panels.

title: Add cache warming via change-data-capture to keep a search index cache hot in a logistics platform
business_context: A logistics platform caches denormalized shipment documents for a search UI. Documents are invalidated on any write, and because shipments update frequently (scans every few minutes), cache entries are constantly cold; hit rate sits at 22% and the search backend (Elasticsearch) is overloaded re-fetching documents that were just invalidated and immediately re-requested.
constraints: Frequently-updated documents must still be cached effectively
Cache must reflect writes within 2 seconds
No polling the DB for changes (use the existing CDC/Debezium stream)
Avoid invalidate-then-cold-miss churn
Existing get_shipment(id) read path unchanged
Backfill must not overwhelm Elasticsearch

before_state.description: Writes invalidate the cache entry; the next read misses and re-fetches from Elasticsearch. For hot, frequently-updated shipments this causes constant invalidate-then-cold-miss churn and a low hit rate.

issues: Invalidate-on-write causes immediate cold miss for hot docs
Hit rate ~22% on frequently-updated shipments
Re-fetch load slams Elasticsearch

reasoning: Naive approach: increase TTL to keep entries longer. Rejected because longer TTL serves stale data for frequently-changing shipments, which the search UI cannot tolerate. Alternative: write-through inside the application write path — rejected because writes come from many services and a scanning microservice that the cache team does not own, so they cannot all be made to write-through. Chosen path: refresh-on-change via the existing CDC stream. A consumer subscribes to the shipment CDC topic; on each change event it rebuilds the cache entry (write-through from the change) instead of merely deleting it, so the entry stays warm and fresh. Cross-layer: any service writes the shipment -> Debezium emits a change event -> the cache-warmer consumer fetches the new doc from ES and SETs the cache entry -> get_shipment reads a warm, fresh entry -> no invalidate-then-miss churn -> tests assert a change event refreshes (not just deletes) the entry and hit rate stays high.

after_state.description: A CDC consumer refreshes cache entries on every shipment change, keeping hot documents warm and fresh instead of deleting and cold-missing. Reads find warm entries, slashing Elasticsearch re-fetch load.

files.purpose: Refreshes cache entries from CDC change events so hot docs stay warm and current
Read path now mostly hits warm entries maintained by the warmer; metrics expose hit rate
Coalesces rapid change bursts per shipment so a hot doc does not hammer Elasticsearch
Asserts CDC events refresh entries and bursts are coalesced to protect Elasticsearch

migration_strategy: Deploy the CDC warmer consuming events but writing to a shadow key namespace
Compare shadow entries against live reads to validate freshness
Switch on_write from delete to no-op and let the warmer own refreshes on canary
Enable warmer-maintained entries for live reads; watch hit rate climb and ES load fall
Add the per-shipment coalescing throttle and tune min_interval
Rollback: revert on_write to delete and stop the warmer if event lag appears
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds an ES document and feeds a CDC change event; assert the cache entry is refreshed with the new status (output + side-effect: SET not DELETE). Hit-rate test: simulate a hot shipment updated frequently and assert reads continue to hit the warm entry. Chaos test: emit 10 rapid events for one shipment and assert the throttle coalesces them to a single ES fetch, and that a dropped CDC event still self-heals via the read-path miss within the TTL.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Raising the TTL keeps entries longer but serves stale shipment state to the search UI, which is unacceptable for frequently-scanned shipments; meanwhile the invalidate-on-write churn for hot documents is exactly what tanks hit rate, and a longer TTL does not address the delete-then-immediate-miss pattern at all.

title: Fix a race where concurrent writers overwrite each other in a read-modify-write counter cache
business_context: A fintech rewards service caches a user's points balance and updates it with a read-modify-write: GET points, add delta, SET points. Under concurrent transactions (two purchases at once), both read the same base, both add, and one SET clobbers the other, so points are lost. Reconciliation found ~$3,100/month of under-credited rewards.
constraints: Concurrent increments must all be reflected (no lost updates)
Operations must remain atomic across multiple API instances
Balance must stay consistent with the Postgres ledger of record
Backward compatible add_points(user, delta) signature
No serialization bottleneck on a single global lock
Idempotency: a retried request must not double-credit

before_state.description: The cache update is a non-atomic read-modify-write. Two concurrent updaters read the same value and the later SET overwrites the earlier increment, silently losing points.

issues: Non-atomic read-modify-write loses concurrent increments
Cache and ledger can diverge on a partial failure
No idempotency: a retry double-credits

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap the read-modify-write in a Redis WATCH/MULTI transaction and retry on conflict. Rejected as the primary fix because under heavy contention WATCH retries thrash and add latency, and it still does not give idempotency or ledger consistency. Alternative: a global per-user lock — rejected because it serializes a hot user's purchases and adds lock-management complexity and deadlock risk. Chosen path: make the cache update atomic with INCRBY (which is inherently lock-free and lost-update-safe) and enforce idempotency with a per-request dedupe key, while treating Postgres as the source of truth via an outbox so the cache and ledger cannot diverge. Cross-layer: addPoints writes the ledger + outbox atomically with an idempotency key -> the worker applies INCRBY to the cache for each unique event -> a dedupe set prevents double-credit on retries -> reads return the atomic counter -> tests assert concurrent increments all land and retries do not double-credit.

after_state.description: The cache counter is updated with atomic INCRBY (no lost updates), idempotency keys prevent double-crediting on retries, and the ledger-plus-outbox keeps the cache consistent with the source of truth.

files.purpose: Atomic ledger write with idempotency and an outbox event; no racy cache write in the request path
Applies atomic INCRBY exactly once per event, deduped by a Redis applied-set
Asserts all concurrent increments land and idempotent retries do not double-credit

migration_strategy: Add the idempotency column, outbox table, and worker without changing the cache write yet
Switch addPoints to ledger+outbox while keeping the legacy RMW disabled behind a flag
Backfill the cache counter from the ledger sum once before enabling INCRBY updates
Enable worker INCRBY updates on canary users; verify totals match the ledger
Ramp to all users; remove the legacy read-modify-write path
Rollback: re-point reads at a ledger-sum computation if the cache counter is suspected wrong
testing_strategy: Fixture fires 50 concurrent addPoints with distinct idempotency keys, runs the worker, and asserts the counter equals the exact sum (output: no lost updates). Idempotency test: call addPoints twice with the same key and assert the balance reflects a single credit (side-effect: applied-set blocks the duplicate). Chaos test: kill the worker mid-batch and restart; assert the applied-set prevents re-applying already-credited events so the counter is still exact.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The GET/compute/SET sequence is not atomic, so two concurrent purchases both read the same base balance, both compute base+delta, and the second SET overwrites the first — the first increment vanishes; WATCH retries reduce but do not eliminate the thrash and still leave the cache able to diverge from the ledger.

title: Replace per-request JWT permission lookups with a short-TTL cache plus revocation list
business_context: A healthcare API checks user permissions on every request by querying an authz service (35ms). At 9k req/s this adds 35ms to p50 and overloads the authz service, which started returning 503s during peak. Permissions rarely change, but when access is revoked it must take effect quickly for compliance.
constraints: Revocation must take effect within 10 seconds (compliance requirement)
Cached permissions must reduce authz QPS by >90%
A stale-but-cached permission must never outlive a revocation beyond the window
Auth context must remain correct under multi-instance deployment
Existing checkPermission(userId, action) signature unchanged
Fail-closed: on cache+authz failure, deny rather than allow

before_state.description: Every request synchronously calls the authz service. There is no caching, so identical permission checks repeat constantly and the authz service is the bottleneck.

issues: No caching: authz called on every request
35ms added to p50 and authz overloaded
No revocation mechanism beyond the live call

reasoning: Naive approach: cache permissions with a long TTL. Rejected because a long TTL means a revoked user keeps access until the TTL expires, violating the 10s compliance window. Alternative: cache with a very short (5s) TTL only — rejected because at 9k req/s with many distinct users the authz QPS reduction is modest and synchronized expiries re-spike authz. Chosen path: a short-TTL positive cache backed by an explicit revocation list. Permissions cache for 60s for low authz load, but every request also checks a fast revocation set (revoked:userId) that is published on revocation; a revoked user is denied immediately regardless of the cached allow. Fail-closed on errors. Cross-layer: checkPermission consults the local cache -> if revoked-set contains the user, deny -> else use cached allow or fetch+cache from authz -> revocation publishes to the revoked set + pub/sub to evict caches -> tests assert >90% authz reduction and revocation within the window.

after_state.description: Permissions are cached for 60s to cut authz QPS, but a fast revocation set is checked on every request and updated on revocation, so revoked access takes effect within seconds. Errors fail closed.

files.purpose: Short-TTL permission cache with a revocation check and fail-closed semantics
Maintains a fast revocation set and broadcasts cache eviction on revoke
Asserts authz QPS reduction and immediate effect of revocation over a cached allow

migration_strategy: Deploy the cache + revocation set with PERM_CACHE=false (still per-request authz)
Enable the revocation subscriber and verify pub/sub eviction works
Turn on PERM_CACHE on canary; verify authz QPS drops >90% and checks stay correct
Test a revocation end-to-end and confirm denial within 10s
Ramp to fleet; alert if authz error rate or fail-closed denials spike
Rollback: PERM_CACHE=false reverts to per-request authz evaluation
testing_strategy: Fixture stubs authz.evaluate with a counter; 1000 repeated checks assert a single authz call (side-effect: QPS cut). Revocation test: cache an allow, call revoke, and assert the next check returns false (output) and that the revoked set contains the user. Chaos test: make both the cache and authz throw and assert checkPermission returns false (fail-closed), never granting access on error.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A long-TTL permission cache keeps serving a cached allow for a user whose access was revoked until the TTL expires, blowing the 10-second compliance window; the revocation simply has no path to take effect because nothing consults a revocation signal on the hot path.

title: Eliminate cache serialization overhead by switching from JSON to a binary codec in a high-QPS Go cache
business_context: A Go ad-serving service caches campaign objects in Redis. At 200k req/s, JSON marshal/unmarshal of the campaign struct dominates CPU profiles (28% of total) and adds GC pressure that causes periodic 15ms STW pauses, hurting the 5ms latency SLO for bid responses.
constraints: Serialization must be significantly cheaper than encoding/json
Schema may evolve (add fields) without breaking old cached entries
Must avoid unbounded allocations per request (reduce GC pressure)
Backward compatible Get(id) returning a Campaign struct
Cache payload size should not grow
Decode must be safe against malformed/truncated data

before_state.description: Campaign structs are JSON-encoded into Redis. At 200k req/s the marshal/unmarshal cost and the allocations it produces dominate CPU and cause GC pauses that breach the latency SLO.

issues: encoding/json marshal/unmarshal dominates CPU at 200k qps
High per-request allocations increase GC pressure and STW pauses
Reflection-based codec is slow on the hot path

reasoning: Naive approach: switch to jsoniter for a faster JSON path. Rejected because it reduces but does not remove reflection and still allocates heavily per decode; the GC pressure persists. Alternative: gob encoding — rejected because gob is Go-specific, not schema-evolution friendly, and still reflection-heavy. Chosen path: code-generated Protobuf (or flatbuffers) for the campaign type, which gives zero-reflection marshal/unmarshal, smaller payloads, forward/backward-compatible field evolution, and far fewer allocations. We define a proto schema, generate Go bindings, and map to/from the internal struct (or use the generated type directly). Cross-layer: Set marshals via generated proto (no reflection) -> Redis stores compact bytes -> Get unmarshals via generated proto with bounded allocation -> schema can add fields safely -> malformed bytes return an error treated as a miss -> tests assert round-trip equality, payload size, and zero unmarshal errors on schema add.

after_state.description: Campaigns are serialized with code-generated Protobuf, eliminating reflection and slashing allocations and payload size while supporting safe field evolution. Decode errors are treated as a miss.

files.purpose: Schema for zero-reflection, evolvable binary serialization of the campaign
Hot-path marshal/unmarshal via generated proto with safe handling of malformed data
Asserts correct binary round-trip and that malformed bytes degrade to a miss

migration_strategy: Generate proto bindings and add Set/Get proto paths behind PROTO_CACHE=false
Dual-write JSON and proto entries under distinct key suffixes to validate parity
Benchmark CPU and GC pauses with proto on a canary at production QPS
Switch reads to proto-first with JSON fallback on canary
Ramp to 100%; drop JSON writes and let JSON keys TTL out
Rollback: PROTO_CACHE=false reverts to JSON (dual-written entries still readable)
testing_strategy: Fixture round-trips a Campaign through proto Set/Get and asserts field equality (output). Allocation/perf test: a benchmark asserts proto Unmarshal allocates fewer bytes/op than the JSON baseline (side-effect: reduced allocation). Chaos/negative test: store truncated/garbage bytes and assert Get returns ErrCacheMiss rather than a panic or partially-decoded struct, proving safe decode.
what_breaks_naive_approach: jsoniter speeds up parsing but is still reflection-based and allocates a fresh object graph per decode, so at 200k req/s the allocation rate and resulting GC STW pauses persist and keep breaching the 5ms latency SLO; only removing reflection and cutting allocations addresses the GC pressure.

title: Add per-key adaptive TTL to a recommendation cache to balance freshness and hit rate
business_context: A streaming service caches per-user recommendations with a uniform 10-minute TTL. Power users (who browse constantly) get stale recs that hurt engagement, while casual users trigger expensive recompute on every visit because their entry always expired. A flat TTL serves neither well: engagement on recs dropped 8% and recompute cost is high.
constraints: Active users should get fresher recs; rarely-active users should keep cached recs longer
Recompute is expensive (1.1s ML inference)
TTL must adapt without a separate scheduling system
No stampede when many entries expire
Existing getRecs(userId) signature unchanged
TTL bounds: 2 min minimum, 6 hours maximum

before_state.description: A uniform TTL is applied to every user. It is simultaneously too long for active users (stale recs) and too short for casual users (constant expensive recompute), and uniform TTLs cause synchronized expiry.

issues: Flat TTL is stale for active users and wasteful for casual users
Uniform TTL causes synchronized expiry storms
No per-user adaptation

reasoning: Naive approach: shorten the TTL globally for freshness. Rejected because it multiplies expensive ML recompute for casual users and worsens stampede. Alternative: maintain a background scheduler that recomputes active users' recs on a cadence — rejected as operationally heavy and hard to keep in sync with actual activity. Chosen path: adaptive per-key TTL derived from the user's activity rate. Track a lightweight recency signal (last-access timestamp / access frequency) and set the TTL inversely to activity: highly active users get a short TTL (fresh, and they re-warm it anyway), inactive users get a long TTL (avoid wasteful recompute). Add jitter to prevent synchronized expiry. Cross-layer: getRecs reads activity score -> computes adaptive TTL within bounds -> sets cache with jittered adaptive TTL -> active users naturally keep entries warm -> tests assert active vs casual users get different TTLs and recompute cost drops.

after_state.description: TTL is computed per user from an activity score: active users get short, fresh TTLs (which they re-warm), casual users get long TTLs to avoid wasteful recompute. Jitter prevents synchronized expiry.

files.purpose: Maps a user activity score to a jittered, bounded TTL balancing freshness and cost
Maintains a cheap decaying activity counter used to drive the adaptive TTL
Applies the per-user adaptive TTL on cache writes
Asserts the inverse relationship and that TTLs stay within bounds

migration_strategy: Deploy activity tracking (recordAccess) without changing TTL behavior
Enable adaptiveTtl behind ADAPTIVE_TTL=false (still flat 600s)
Turn on adaptive TTL for a canary cohort; compare recompute rate and rec freshness
Tune the activity-to-TTL curve and bounds from observed engagement
Ramp to 100%; confirm recompute cost down for casual users and fresher recs for active users
Rollback: ADAPTIVE_TTL=false reverts to the flat TTL
testing_strategy: Fixture computes adaptiveTtl for a high activity score and a zero score and asserts the active TTL is shorter and both are within [120, 21660] (output). Behavior test: simulate an active user (many recordAccess) and assert getRecs writes a short TTL while a casual user gets a long TTL (side-effect: differing EX values). Chaos test: with the activity counter unavailable (Redis error), assert adaptiveTtl falls back to a safe default TTL rather than throwing, so recs are still cached.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A globally shorter TTL forces casual users' entries to expire before their next visit, so each casual visit re-runs the 1.1s ML inference, exploding recompute cost; and the still-uniform expiry keeps producing synchronized expiry storms — the flat TTL cannot serve both user populations at once.

title: Fix a CDN cache that caches error responses, amplifying an origin incident into a long outage
business_context: During a brief origin database blip, the origin returned 500s for ~90 seconds. The CDN cached those 500 responses with the default 60s TTL (no special handling), so even after the database recovered, the CDN kept serving cached 500s to users for the remainder of the TTL — turning a 90-second origin incident into a 4-minute user-visible outage for cached paths.
constraints: Error responses (5xx) must never be cached, or cached only very briefly
Successful responses must still cache normally for performance
A recovered origin must be reflected to users within seconds
Must not stampede the origin when error caching is disabled (negative TTL discipline)
Works at the edge without origin code changes for the cache rule
Distinguish transient 5xx from cacheable 404s

before_state.description: The edge caches whatever the origin returns with a uniform TTL, including 5xx error responses. After the origin recovers, users keep getting cached errors until the TTL expires.

issues: Caches 5xx responses for 60s, prolonging incidents
No status-aware TTL policy
Recovered origin not reflected until TTL expiry

reasoning: Naive approach: never cache anything but 200s. Rejected because some non-200s (e.g. 404, 301) are legitimately cacheable and skipping them all reduces hit rate and can stampede the origin with repeated 404 probes. Alternative: serve stale-on-error so a 5xx falls back to the last good cached response — good, but on its own it does not prevent caching the fresh 5xx. Chosen path: status-aware caching policy combined with serve-stale-if-error. Cache 2xx normally; do not cache 5xx at all (or with a 1s negative TTL to dampen bursts); cache 404 briefly. When the origin returns 5xx, serve the last good cached entry if available (stale-if-error). Cross-layer: edge fetches origin -> branch on status -> 2xx cache normally -> 5xx never stored, and serve stale if present else pass the error -> 404 short TTL -> recovered origin's first 2xx repopulates immediately -> tests assert 5xx is not cached and recovery is immediate.

after_state.description: The edge applies a status-aware policy: 2xx caches normally, 5xx is never cached (serving last-good stale if available), and 404 caches briefly. A recovered origin is reflected on the next request.

files.purpose: Status-aware caching with stale-if-error so 5xx is never cached and recovery is immediate
Asserts 5xx is not cached, stale-if-error serves last good, and recovery is immediate

migration_strategy: Deploy the status-aware rule to a preview/edge canary
Replay a synthetic 5xx burst and confirm no 5xx is cached
Verify stale-if-error serves the last good response during the burst
Confirm a recovered origin is reflected on the very next request
Roll to 100% of edge traffic and monitor error-cache-hit metric (should be zero)
Rollback: revert to the prior cache rule (accepting the known 5xx-caching risk)
testing_strategy: Fixture makes the origin return 500 and asserts the edge cache has no entry for the path (side-effect: 5xx not stored). Stale-if-error test: cache a 200 'good', then make origin 500 and assert the user still receives 'good' (output). Recovery test: after a 5xx the next 200 is served immediately. Chaos test: a sustained 5xx storm with no prior cached entry asserts the edge passes errors through without ever caching them, so recovery is instant once the origin heals.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Caching only 200s drops legitimately cacheable 404/redirect responses, hurting hit rate and letting repeated 404 probes stampede the origin; it also still does not provide a stale fallback during a 5xx, so although the incident is not prolonged, users see raw errors instead of the last good content.

title: Replace a global cache flush on deploy with surgical invalidation to avoid post-deploy DB storms
business_context: A B2B reporting app runs FLUSHDB on every deploy 'to be safe', wiping all caches. With 4 deploys/day and a warm cache that normally absorbs 85% of reads, each deploy causes a 3-minute window where the cold cache forwards all reads to Postgres, which hits 90% CPU and slows reports for every tenant. Deploys have become a recurring mini-incident.
constraints: Deploys must not cold-start the entire cache
Only entries whose data shape or logic actually changed should be invalidated
Backward compatible cache keys for unchanged data
Must work across many tenants without per-tenant downtime
No manual key bookkeeping per deploy
DB CPU must stay under 60% during deploys

before_state.description: The deploy pipeline runs FLUSHDB unconditionally, discarding the entire warm cache even when most cached data and logic are unchanged, forcing a full cold start every deploy.

files.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
redis-cli -h $REDIS_HOST FLUSHDB   # nukes everything every deploy
issues: FLUSHDB wipes the entire warm cache on every deploy
Cold cache forwards all reads to Postgres for minutes
Unchanged data is needlessly recomputed
Keys carry no logic/version namespace
Cannot invalidate only the reports whose computation changed

reasoning: Naive approach: keep FLUSHDB but warm the cache after deploy. Rejected because warming the whole keyspace is itself a stampede and most of it was still valid, so the flush+warm is pure waste. Alternative: track changed keys per deploy manually — rejected as error-prone bookkeeping that drifts from reality. Chosen path: version the cache namespace by logic version. Each report type's cache key includes a logic-version constant bumped only when that report's computation actually changes; a deploy that does not change a report's logic leaves its keys valid and warm. Invalidation becomes implicit: bumping a logic version orphans only the affected entries, no FLUSHDB. Cross-layer: build embeds per-report LOGIC_VERSION -> reportKey includes the version -> a deploy that changes report X bumps only X's version -> unchanged reports keep serving warm entries -> orphaned old-version entries TTL out -> tests assert unchanged keys survive a deploy and changed-logic keys are invalidated.

after_state.description: Cache keys embed a per-report logic version. A deploy bumps only the versions of reports whose computation changed, so unchanged data stays warm and no global flush is needed.

files.purpose: Single source of truth for per-report logic versions, edited only when logic changes
Embeds the logic version so a bump invalidates only that report type's entries

files.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# No FLUSHDB. Invalidation is implicit via logic-version bumps in code.
# Old-version keys orphan and TTL out automatically.
echo "deploy complete: cache preserved, only bumped report versions invalidated"
files.purpose: Removes the global flush; invalidation is handled by version-scoped keys
Asserts unchanged reports stay warm and changed reports are invalidated by version

migration_strategy: Introduce version-scoped keys with all versions starting at 1 and stop FLUSHDB in the pipeline
Deploy once with versions matching current logic so the warm cache is preserved
On the next release, bump only the changed report's version and verify selective invalidation
Monitor DB CPU across a few deploys to confirm no cold-start spikes
Document the version-bump step in the release checklist
Rollback: re-add FLUSHDB to the pipeline if a logic-version mismatch is suspected
testing_strategy: Fixture asserts reportKey for an unchanged report type yields a stable key across releases (output: warm entries survive) and that a bumped report type yields a new key (side-effect: old entries orphaned). Deploy-simulation test: warm several report keys, simulate a deploy that bumps one report type, and assert only that type's keys miss while the rest hit. Chaos test: a report type missing from LOGIC_VERSIONS defaults to v0 deterministically rather than throwing, so a forgotten entry degrades safely.
what_breaks_naive_approach: FLUSHDB-then-warm discards the entire warm cache and then re-warming the full keyspace is itself a coordinated stampede on Postgres; since most cached data and logic were unchanged, the flush throws away valid entries and recreates the very cold-start DB storm the team is trying to avoid.

title: Add a Bloom filter in front of a cache to stop pointless lookups for known-absent keys
business_context: A gaming backend checks whether a username is taken on every keystroke in a signup form. Most checks are for names that do not exist. Each check is a cache miss (negative caching helps for repeats but not first-time distinct names) followed by a DB existence query. During a popular game launch, ~50k distinct first-time checks/s flood Postgres with existence queries, spiking its CPU.
constraints: Must short-circuit checks for usernames that definitely do not exist
Must never falsely report a taken name as available (no false negatives)
Bloom filter must be updated when a new username is registered
Memory for the filter must be bounded (millions of usernames)
Existing isTaken(username) -> boolean signature preserved
Filter must be shared/consistent across instances

before_state.description: Every existence check that misses the cache hits the DB. First-time distinct usernames are never cached, so a flood of unique checks bypasses the cache straight to Postgres.

issues: First-time distinct usernames always hit the DB
Existence queries flood Postgres during launch
No structure to cheaply rule out absent names

reasoning: Naive approach: cache negatives aggressively. Rejected because first-time distinct checks have no prior negative entry, so the flood of unique names still hits the DB; negative caching only helps repeats. Alternative: load all usernames into a Redis SET and SISMEMBER — rejected because at tens of millions of usernames the SET is large and SISMEMBER still needs a network round-trip per check. Chosen path: a Bloom filter of existing usernames (e.g. RedisBloom or an in-process filter synced from it). A Bloom filter answers 'definitely not present' with no false negatives; if the filter says absent, the name is available without touching the DB; if it says possibly present, fall through to the cache/DB to confirm. Registration adds the name to the filter. Cross-layer: isTaken queries the Bloom filter -> definitely-absent returns available immediately (no DB) -> possibly-present falls to cache then DB -> registration BF.ADD updates the filter -> filter shared via RedisBloom across instances -> tests assert no false negatives and that absent checks skip the DB.

after_state.description: A Bloom filter of existing usernames short-circuits the common 'name is free' case with no DB query and zero false negatives. Only possibly-present names fall through to the cache/DB, and registration updates the filter.

files.purpose: Bloom membership test that definitively rules out absent usernames without a DB query
Short-circuits the available-name path via the filter, reserving DB checks for possible collisions
Adds newly registered usernames to the filter so future checks are accurate
Asserts absent checks avoid the DB and that registered names are never falsely available

migration_strategy: Build the Bloom filter by backfilling all existing usernames via BF.ADD in a one-off job
Deploy isTaken with the filter behind BLOOM=false (still DB-on-miss)
Enable BLOOM on canary; verify DB existence QPS drops and no false negatives in audit
Wire registration to BF.ADD so the filter stays current
Ramp to 100% and monitor Postgres CPU during a synthetic launch load
Rollback: BLOOM=false reverts to cache+DB checks (filter stays maintained for re-enable)
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds the filter with known usernames and stubs db.usernameExists with a counter. Assert a definitely-absent name returns false with dbCalls === 0 (side-effect: DB skipped). Correctness test: register a name and assert isTaken returns true (no false negative). Chaos test: simulate a Bloom false positive (filter says possibly-present for an absent name) and assert isTaken still returns the correct false after the DB confirm, proving false positives are merely a performance, not correctness, cost.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Negative caching only short-circuits repeated checks of the same name; during a launch the flood is dominated by first-time distinct username checks that have no negative entry, so each one still runs a DB existence query and Postgres is overwhelmed regardless of how good the negative cache is for repeats.

title: Fix a multi-layer cache where L1 and L2 disagree, serving inconsistent data after writes
business_context: A SaaS uses an in-process L1 cache and a Redis L2. On write, only L2 is invalidated; L1 entries on each instance keep serving the old value until their independent TTL expires. Users hitting different instances see different values for the same record (one updated, one stale) for up to 30 seconds, generating confused support tickets and a perception of flaky data.
constraints: L1 and L2 must converge after a write within 2 seconds across all instances
L1 must not be removed (it is needed for sub-millisecond reads)
Writes can originate from any instance
No per-read L2 round-trip when L1 is warm
Existing get(key)/set(key,val) API unchanged
Must tolerate an instance missing an invalidation message (bounded staleness backstop)

before_state.description: Writes invalidate L2 only. Each instance's L1 keeps serving stale data until its own TTL expires, so different instances disagree after a write.

issues: set invalidates L2 but not other instances' L1 caches
Different instances disagree after a write for up to L1 TTL
No cross-instance L1 invalidation channel

reasoning: Naive approach: drop L1 TTL to 1s. Rejected because a 1s L1 TTL guts the hit rate L1 exists to provide and causes frequent synchronized L2 reads. Alternative: make every L1 read validate against L2 with a version check — rejected because that adds an L2 round-trip per read, defeating L1's sub-ms purpose. Chosen path: cross-instance L1 invalidation via Redis Pub/Sub on write, with a version-stamped backstop. On set, invalidate L2 and PUBLISH the key; every instance's subscriber evicts that key from L1 so all converge within pub/sub latency. A monotonic version per key (checked lazily, cheaply) backstops any missed message so staleness is bounded. Cross-layer: set writes DB, dels L2, publishes key -> each instance subscriber evicts L1[key] -> next read repopulates from L2 -> version backstop bounds missed-message staleness -> tests assert all instances converge within 2s and a dropped message still self-heals.

after_state.description: Writes invalidate L2 and broadcast the key so every instance evicts its L1 entry, converging within pub/sub latency. A per-key version provides a bounded-staleness backstop if a message is missed.

files.purpose: Write path updates L2 with a version and broadcasts an L1 eviction to all instances
Subscribes to evictions so every instance drops the stale L1 entry on a write
Asserts cross-instance convergence on write and bounded staleness on a dropped message

migration_strategy: Deploy the pub/sub invalidator and version stamping behind L1_PUBSUB=false
Enable subscribers everywhere (consuming evictions) before enabling publishing
Turn on publishing on writes for a canary; verify two instances converge after a write
Measure convergence latency against the 2s target
Ramp to fleet; keep L1 TTL as the missed-message backstop
Rollback: L1_PUBSUB=false reverts to L2-only invalidation (accepting the known divergence)
testing_strategy: Fixture builds two instances, warms both L1 caches with an old value, writes on one, drains pub/sub, and asserts the other instance reads the new value (output: convergence; side-effect: L1 eviction). Backstop test: drop pub/sub for an instance, perform an external write, and assert the instance reflects the new value after the TTL backstop. Chaos test: flood writes to the same key and assert no instance ever serves a value older than the last write beyond the backstop window.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Invalidating only L2 leaves each instance's L1 serving the pre-write value until that L1's independent TTL expires, so two users on two instances see different values for the same record; shrinking the L1 TTL to mask this destroys L1's hit rate and reintroduces synchronized L2 read bursts.

title: Add cache key namespacing to prevent collisions between two services sharing one Redis
business_context: Two services (an orders service and an offers service) share a Redis instance to cut cost. Both independently used keys like id:12345. A collision occurred where the offers service overwrote an order's cached payload, and the orders service then deserialized an offer object and returned a malformed order to a customer. The incident corrupted ~120 order responses before detection.
constraints: Each service's keyspace must be isolated to prevent collisions
Migration must not lose currently-cached data abruptly
Backward compatible read paths during transition
No separate Redis instance (cost constraint stands)
Keys must remain debuggable/greppable per service
Cross-service ops (shared rate-limit keys) must still work intentionally

before_state.description: Both services write unprefixed keys into the same Redis. Identical id-based keys collide, so one service's value can overwrite the other's, producing cross-type corruption.

issues: Unprefixed key id:* collides with the offers service
Cross-type overwrite corrupts order responses
No namespace isolation
Shares the id:* keyspace with orders
Can overwrite an order's cached value
No service prefix

reasoning: Naive approach: just rename keys in each service ad hoc. Rejected because uncoordinated renames risk new collisions and abrupt cache loss when both deploy, and there is no enforced convention to prevent regressions. Alternative: move one service to a separate Redis — rejected by the cost constraint. Chosen path: a centralized, enforced key-namespacing convention. Introduce a tiny key-builder per service that prefixes every key with a service namespace (orders: / offers:), route all cache access through it, and keep an explicit shared: namespace for intentional cross-service keys. Migration dual-reads old and new keys so warm data is not lost. Cross-layer: each service's cache module uses ns('orders', ...) -> all keys carry a service prefix -> shared keys use ns('shared', ...) deliberately -> a lint/test forbids raw redis.get/set bypassing the builder -> tests assert no key produced by one service can match another's.

after_state.description: All cache access goes through a per-service key builder that namespaces keys by service, with an explicit shared namespace for intentional cross-service keys. A guard prevents raw key usage, eliminating collisions.

files.purpose: Single enforced key builder that namespaces every key by service
Namespaces order keys and dual-reads the legacy key during migration
Namespaces offer keys, isolating them from the orders service
Asserts isolation between services and rejection of unknown namespaces

migration_strategy: Introduce the ns key builder and route both services' cache access through it (writing new keys)
Keep a transitional read fallback to the legacy id:* key so warm data is not lost
Add a lint rule/test forbidding raw redis.get/set with unprefixed keys
Let legacy id:* keys TTL out as namespaced keys take over
Remove the legacy fallback once no id:* hits remain
Rollback: re-enable the legacy fallback read if any namespaced key is missing
testing_strategy: Fixture asserts ns('orders',...) and ns('offers',...) produce distinct keys for the same id (output: isolation) and that writing an order then reading an offer with the same id returns null/own-namespace, never the other's value (side-effect: no cross-overwrite). Negative test: ns with an unknown service throws. Chaos test: concurrently write an order and an offer with the same numeric id and assert each reads back its own correctly-typed object.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Ad-hoc renaming without a shared enforced convention lets a future code path reintroduce an unprefixed key, re-creating the cross-service overwrite; and renaming both services simultaneously without a dual-read drops all warm cache at once, causing a DB load spike on top of the original corruption risk.

title: Replace synchronous cache rebuild with async refresh-ahead to remove tail-latency spikes in a Nuxt API
business_context: A Nuxt server-API caches a 600ms-to-compute homepage payload. When the entry expires, the unlucky request that triggers the rebuild waits the full 600ms, so a predictable slice of users see a 600ms+ TTFB every TTL cycle. At peak this is ~150 users per minute experiencing the slow path, dragging the p99 to 720ms against a 250ms SLO.
constraints: No user request should block on the full rebuild
Only one rebuild per key should run at a time
Served data may be slightly stale (a few seconds) during refresh
Refresh must be triggered before the entry is fully stale (refresh-ahead)
Existing getHomepage() signature unchanged
Must not refresh excessively (bounded refresh frequency)

before_state.description: The cache rebuilds synchronously on expiry, so the request that hits the expired entry pays the full 600ms compute, producing a recurring tail-latency spike each TTL cycle.

issues: The request that hits expiry blocks on the full 600ms rebuild
Recurring p99 spike every TTL cycle
No refresh-ahead or single-flight

reasoning: Naive approach: increase the TTL so rebuilds are rarer. Rejected because it only reduces the frequency of the spike, not its existence; the unlucky request still eats 600ms, and the data gets staler. Alternative: a cron/background job that rebuilds on a fixed schedule — rejected because it rebuilds even when no one is asking (waste) and can drift from real traffic patterns and TTL. Chosen path: refresh-ahead with single-flight. Store value plus a 'softExpire' timestamp earlier than the hard TTL. When a request reads an entry past softExpire but before hard expiry, it serves the still-valid value immediately and kicks off a single background rebuild (guarded by a lock). Users never block; the entry is refreshed before it goes fully stale. Cross-layer: getHomepage reads {data, softExpire} -> fresh: return -> past softExpire: return data + async single-flight rebuild -> rebuild updates value and timestamps -> hard TTL backstops a fully cold case -> tests assert the user response is fast and exactly one rebuild runs.

after_state.description: Entries carry a soft-expire earlier than the hard TTL. A read past soft-expire serves the current value instantly and triggers a single background rebuild, so users never block on the 600ms compute.

files.purpose: Serves cached data instantly and triggers a background refresh past soft-expire
Single-flight background rebuild with soft/hard expiry; only cold keys block
Asserts non-blocking serve and exactly one background rebuild

migration_strategy: Deploy refresh-ahead with SOFT set equal to HARD (behaves like the old path) behind REFRESH_AHEAD=false
Enable on canary with SOFT < HARD; verify the rebuild moves off the request path
Confirm single-flight prevents concurrent rebuilds
Ramp to 100%; watch p99 drop toward the SLO
Tune SOFT to keep entries fresh without excessive refreshes
Rollback: REFRESH_AHEAD=false reverts to synchronous rebuild
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds an entry already past soft-expire and a 600ms build counter. Assert getHomepage returns in <50ms (output: non-blocking) and that exactly one background build runs (side-effect). Single-flight test: 100 concurrent reads past soft-expire trigger only one build. Chaos test: make the background build throw and assert the cached value keeps being served (no user error) until a later successful refresh, and the hard TTL still backstops a fully cold key.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Raising the TTL only spaces out the rebuilds; the request that lands on the expired entry still synchronously computes the 600ms payload, so the recurring p99 spike persists (just less often) and the cached data is staler between rebuilds — the blocking rebuild itself is never removed from the user path.

title: Fix cache invalidation that misses derived/aggregate keys after a base entity write
business_context: An e-commerce admin updates a product, which invalidates the product cache. But several derived caches depend on it: the category listing, the search facet counts, and the 'related products' block. None are invalidated, so after a price/availability edit the product page is correct while the category page and facets show stale data for up to an hour, confusing shoppers and triggering 'price mismatch' complaints.
constraints: A base entity write must invalidate all caches derived from it
Dependency graph between caches must be explicit and maintainable
Invalidation must be complete but not over-broad (avoid flushing unrelated caches)
Backward compatible product write path
Derived recomputation must not stampede on bulk edits
Dependencies may be many-to-many (a product affects multiple categories)

before_state.description: The write path invalidates only the product's own cache key. Derived caches (category listings, facet counts, related blocks) that depend on the product are left stale because nothing tracks those dependencies.

issues: Only the product's own cache is invalidated
Category listing, facet counts, related-products caches stay stale
No dependency tracking between caches

reasoning: Naive approach: also delete a hardcoded list of derived keys in updateProduct. Rejected because the derived-key list drifts: new derived caches get added elsewhere and the write path forgets them, recreating the stale-derived bug. Alternative: flush all listing/facet caches on any product write — rejected as over-broad, causing needless recompute stampedes on bulk edits. Chosen path: an explicit dependency registry plus tag-based invalidation. Each derived cache declares the tags it depends on (e.g. category:electronics, facets:brand). A product write resolves the product's tags (its categories, brands) and invalidates exactly those tags via generation bumps, orphaning only the affected derived entries. Cross-layer: updateProduct resolves affected tags -> bump each tag's generation (O(1)) -> derived caches keyed by tag generations orphan precisely -> bulk edits coalesce tag bumps -> tests assert all derived caches refresh and unrelated ones are untouched.

after_state.description: Derived caches are keyed by the tags they depend on. A product write resolves and bumps exactly the affected tags, invalidating precisely the dependent derived caches (category, facets, related) without over-broad flushing.

files.purpose: Resolves a product's affected tags and bumps their generations in one pipeline
Invalidates the product and all of its derived caches via tag bumps
Derived cache keyed by tag generation so a tag bump invalidates it automatically
Asserts precise invalidation of dependent caches and no over-broad flushing

migration_strategy: Introduce tag-generation keys for derived caches behind TAG_DERIVED=false
Shadow-bump tags on writes while still serving legacy derived keys to validate mapping
Enable tag-keyed derived reads on canary; verify category/facet caches refresh on product edits
Confirm unrelated derived caches are untouched (no over-invalidation)
Ramp to 100%; legacy derived keys TTL out
Rollback: TAG_DERIVED=false reverts to legacy derived keys (and the known staleness)
testing_strategy: Fixture warms a category listing, updates a product in that category, and asserts the listing's composed key changes (side-effect: derived cache invalidated) and a re-read returns fresh data (output). Precision test: updating a product not in category c2 leaves c2's listing key unchanged (no over-invalidation). Chaos test: bulk-edit 500 products in one category and assert the category tag is bumped a bounded number of times (coalesced) rather than triggering 500 independent derived recomputes simultaneously.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A hardcoded list of derived keys in the write path inevitably falls out of date as new derived caches are introduced elsewhere, so the next added aggregate (a new facet or recommendation block) is silently left stale after writes — the exact derived-cache staleness bug returns whenever the dependency list and the actual dependents diverge.

title: Add gzip + chunked decode to a large-object cache to fix Redis bandwidth saturation
business_context: A mapping service caches large GeoJSON tiles (avg 800KB) in Redis. At 4k tile reads/s, raw payloads push Redis network egress to 95% of the 10Gbps NIC, causing queueing and latency jitter on all Redis ops (including small unrelated keys). The bottleneck is bandwidth, not CPU.
constraints: Redis egress must drop well below the NIC limit
Decompression CPU overhead must be acceptable on the read path
Tiles are immutable once generated (content-addressed)
Backward compatible getTile(z,x,y) signature
Must not corrupt partially-stored large values
Small-key latency must recover (no head-of-line blocking)

before_state.description: GeoJSON tiles are stored uncompressed. At 4k reads/s the 800KB payloads saturate the Redis NIC, causing queueing that adds latency to every Redis operation including small keys.

issues: Uncompressed 800KB tiles saturate Redis network egress
Bandwidth saturation queues all Redis ops, hurting small-key latency
No compression despite highly compressible GeoJSON

reasoning: Naive approach: shard tiles across more Redis nodes to spread bandwidth. Rejected because GeoJSON is highly compressible (~6x), so compression solves the root cause far more cheaply than horizontally scaling NICs, and sharding adds operational cost. Alternative: store tiles in object storage and cache only URLs — rejected because the per-read object-store latency would breach the interactive map's latency budget. Chosen path: gzip-compress tiles before caching and decompress on read. GeoJSON compresses ~6x, cutting egress proportionally; decompression CPU is modest relative to the bandwidth saved. Tiles are immutable/content-addressed so there is no invalidation complexity. A magic-byte prefix lets reads detect compressed vs legacy uncompressed entries during migration. Cross-layer: getTile reads buffer -> detect gzip magic -> gunzip then parse -> writes gzip the JSON -> Redis egress drops ~6x -> small-key latency recovers as the NIC unsaturates -> tests assert round-trip and reduced payload size.

after_state.description: Tiles are gzip-compressed before caching and decompressed on read, cutting Redis egress roughly sixfold and relieving NIC saturation. A magic-byte check supports a smooth migration from legacy uncompressed entries.

files.purpose: Gzip codec with magic-byte detection for compressed vs legacy entries
Stores compressed tiles and transparently decodes on read
Asserts compressed round-trip with size reduction and legacy compatibility

migration_strategy: Deploy the codec with reads handling both compressed and legacy entries
Enable compressed writes on canary; verify Redis egress drops and decode works
Monitor read-path CPU to confirm decompression overhead is acceptable
Ramp compressed writes to 100%; legacy uncompressed entries are re-encoded on regeneration
Confirm small-key latency recovers as NIC unsaturates
Rollback: disable compressed writes; reads still handle both formats so nothing breaks
testing_strategy: Fixture encodes an 800KB tile and asserts the compressed buffer is under one-third the raw size (side-effect: bandwidth reduction) and that decode reproduces the original (output). Legacy test: decode an uncompressed buffer correctly. Chaos test: truncate a compressed buffer (simulate a partial store) and assert decodeTile throws a clear error that the cache layer treats as a miss and regenerates, rather than returning corrupt GeoJSON.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sharding across more Redis nodes spreads the bandwidth but still moves the full uncompressed 800KB per read, so total network traffic is unchanged and the per-NIC headroom gain is linear and costly; it ignores that the GeoJSON is ~6x compressible, which is a far cheaper fix for a bandwidth-bound workload.

title: Prevent cache penetration from malicious null-key probing in a fintech account lookup
business_context: A fintech API looks up accounts by an external reference id.
