An attacker discovered that probing random nonexistent reference ids bypasses the cache (misses are not cached) and forces an expensive cross-shard DB lookup each time. They drove ~20k bogus lookups/s, saturating the account DB and degrading legitimate traffic — a cache-penetration attack.
constraints: Must absorb high-rate probing of nonexistent keys without overloading the DB
Must not let an attacker fill the cache with unbounded null entries (memory)
Legitimate newly-created accounts must resolve promptly (short negative TTL)
Input reference ids must be validated/normalized to reject obviously-invalid formats cheaply
Existing lookupAccount(ref) -> account|null preserved
Per-IP rate limiting on lookups to blunt sustained abuse

before_state.description: Misses are not cached and inputs are not validated, so an attacker can probe random reference ids and force a cross-shard DB lookup on every request, penetrating straight through the cache.

issues: Misses not cached -> every bogus probe hits the DB
No input validation to cheaply reject malformed refs
No rate limiting on lookups

reasoning: Naive approach: cache nulls with the same TTL as hits. Rejected because an attacker can probe millions of distinct random refs and fill Redis with null entries (memory DoS), and a long null TTL hides newly created accounts. Alternative: rely solely on per-IP rate limiting — rejected because distributed probing across many IPs evades simple per-IP limits. Chosen path: layered defense — cheap input validation/normalization to reject malformed refs before any cache/DB access, a Bloom filter of valid account refs to short-circuit definite-absences, short-TTL negative caching for refs that pass validation but are absent, plus per-IP rate limiting as a backstop. Cross-layer: lookupAccount validates ref format (reject early) -> Bloom filter rules out definite absences (no DB) -> negative cache with short TTL absorbs repeats -> rate limiter blunts sustained abuse -> Redis eviction policy bounds null memory -> tests assert bogus probes do not reach the DB and valid new accounts still resolve.

after_state.description: Lookups first validate the ref format, consult a Bloom filter of valid refs to rule out absences without a DB hit, use short-TTL negative caching for the rest, and apply per-IP rate limiting — so probing cannot penetrate to the DB or exhaust memory.

files.purpose: Layers validation, Bloom filter, negative caching, and rate limiting to stop penetration
Cheaply rejects obviously-invalid refs before any cache or DB access
Per-IP sliding-window limiter to blunt sustained probing
Asserts malformed refs skip cache/DB and repeated absences are bounded

migration_strategy: Add input validation first (safe, reduces obvious garbage immediately)
Backfill and deploy the Bloom filter of valid refs behind PENETRATION_GUARD=false
Enable short-TTL negative caching and per-IP rate limiting on canary
Verify bogus-probe DB QPS collapses and legitimate lookups are unaffected
Ramp to 100% with Redis maxmemory-policy volatile-ttl to bound null memory
Rollback: PENETRATION_GUARD=false disables the Bloom/negative layers (validation kept)
testing_strategy: Fixture asserts a malformed ref returns null with zero DB and cache calls (output + side-effect). Penetration test: probe the same valid-format-but-absent ref twice and assert at most one DB call (negative cache bound). Memory test: probe 100k distinct random valid-format refs and assert Redis stays under maxmemory via volatile-ttl eviction. Chaos test: simulate distributed probing from 200 IPs and assert the Bloom filter still short-circuits absences so the DB QPS stays bounded even when per-IP limits are individually unhit.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Caching nulls with a long TTL lets an attacker probe millions of distinct random refs, filling Redis with null entries until it evicts real data (a memory DoS), and simultaneously hides any account created during that long negative TTL; per-IP rate limiting alone fails against distributed probing spread thinly across many IPs.
